CHAPTER
00
Basics of Audit
SA 200: Overall Objectives of Independent Auditor &
Conduct of an Audit in Accordance with Standards on Auditing
Overall Objective of Auditor?
In conducting audit of FS, overall objectives of auditor are:
a) To obtain Reasonable Assurance about whether FS are free from material misstatement, whether
to fraud or error, to express opinion whether FS are prepared, in all material respects, as per AFRE
b) To report & communicate as per SAs.
Definitions
Reasonable Assurance: High, but not absolute, level of assurance.
To obtain reasonable assurance, obtain Sufficient & Appropriate Audit Evidence (SAAE).
✓
✓
Sufficiency is measure of quantity of audit evidence affected by ROMM & Quality of audit evide
Appropriateness is measure of Quality of audit evidence i.e. its relevance & its reliability.
✓ Relevance is the logical connection between the purpose of audit procedure & assertion tested
Reliablity depends on nature, source & circumstances in which audit evidence is obtained.
• Reliability increased when obtained from independent sources outside entity.
✓
 Reliability of A.E. generated internally is increased when related controls are effective.
A.E. obtained directly by auditor more reliable than A.E. obtained indirectly or by inference.
Ο A.E. in documentary form more reliable than evidence obtained orally.
 
A.E. provided by original documents more reliable than photocopies or digitised.
Misstatement: Difference between amount, classification, presentation, or disclosure $(a/c/p/d)$
reported FS item & $(a/c/p/d)$ required as per AFRF. Misstatements can arise from error or fraud.
Misstatement considered material if it can influence economic decisions of user of FS.
Applicable financial reporting framework (AFRF):
FRF adopted by mgt & TCWG in preparation & presentation of FS that's acceptable in view of natu
entity & objective of FS or required by law or regulation.
"Fair presentation framework" refer to FRF that requires compliance with framework &:
i) Acknowledges, to provide disclosures beyond specifically required by framework or
ii) Necessary to depart from framework to achieve fair presentation in extremely rare circumst
"Compliance framework" is FRF that requires compliance with framework, but doesn't
acknowledgements in (i) or (ii) above.
Assertions (SA 315):
Representations by mgt embodied in FS, used by auditor to consider different potential misstate
Classes of transactions and events: Occurrence, Completeness, Accuracy, Cut-off, Classification
Account Balance: Existence, Rights & obligations, Completeness & Valuation
Presentation & Disclosure: Mix of above (excluding cut-off & existence)

Those charged with governance (TCWG):
00 Basics of Audit
Person(s) or organisation(s) (e.g., a corporate trustee) responsibile for overseeing strategic direction of
entity & obligations related to accountability of entity. (Audit committee/CFO/Chairman)
Audit Risk
Risk that auditor expresses inappropriate audit opinion when FS are materially misstated.
Audit risk is function of RoMM and detection risk.
Risk of material misstatement (ROMM): Risk that FS are materially misstated prior to audit. This
consists of 2 components, described as follows at assertion level:
ROMM may exist at two levels:
Why we assess RoMM at assertion level?
ROMM at assertion level assessed to determine NTE of FAP to obtain SAAE.
Detection risk: Risk that procedures performed by auditor will not detect a misstatement that exists &
could be material (individually or when aggregated with other misstatements).
Inherent Limitations of Audit (ILA)
Auditor isn't expected & can't reduce audit risk to zero & therefore can't obtain absolute assurance that
FS are free from material misstatement due to fraud or error. This is because of inherent limitations
of audit, which result in most audit evidence being persuasive rather than conclusive.
Nature of financial reporting:
V
Preparation of FS involves judgment by mgt in applying requirements of AFRF to facts & circumstances
of entity. For eg: $A/c$ estimates
Nature of audit procedures:
Possibility that mgt & others don't provide complete info relevant to preparation of FS
Fraud may involve sophisticated & carefully organised schemes designed to conceal it.
Audit is not official investigation. He doesn't have special legal powers eg. search.

00 Basics of Audit
Beperformance: Independent execution of procedures originally performed by entity's internal control.
Analytical Procedures: Evaluating financial info. by study of relationships among financial & non-financial
data.
Inquiry:
Seeking info of knowledgeable persons, both financial & non- financial, within or outside entity.
May range from formal written to informal oral inquiries.
Used extensively throughout audit in addition to other audit procedures.
Evaluating responses is integral part of inquiry process
New
Support
Responses may provide info. not previously possessed or corroborative qudit evidence.
Alternatively, might provide significantly different info. Eg, info. regarding mgt override of controls.
In case of inquiries about mgt intent info. to support mgt's intent may be limited.
Understanding mgt's past history, reasons & ability to pursue specific course of action may provide
relevant info. to support evidence obtained through inquiry.
Auditor may obtain written representations from mgt & TCWG to confirm responses to oral inquiries.
SA 210 Agreeing the Terms of Audit Engagement
Objective
Accept or continue audit only when basis upon which it is to be performed has been agreed, through:
Establishing whether preconditions for audit are present &
Confirming common understanding between auditor & mgt, TCWG of terms of audit engg.
Preconditions of Audit
Determine whether FRF is acceptable [Factors discussed below]
Obtain agreement of mgt that it acknowledges & understands its responsibility:
i) For preparation of FS as per AFRF
ii) For such Internal Control (IC) to prepare FS free from material misstatement, due to fraud or error &
iii) To provide auditor with:
a) Access to all info. relevant to preparation of FS
b) Additional info. that auditor may request for audit &
c) Unrestricted access to persons within entity to obtain audit evidence.
If preconditions not present, discuss with mgt & do not accept proposed audit.
Factors relevant to auditor's determination of acceptability of FRF to be applied in preparation of FS include:
Nature of entity (business enterprise or not for profit organization)
Purpose of FS (prepared to meet common financial info. needs of wide range of users or specific users)
Nature of FS (complete set of FS or single FS)
Whether law or regulation prescribes AFRF.

New
Support

SA 210 Agreeing the Terms of Audit Engagement
Objective
Accept or continue audit only when basis upon which it is to be performed has been agreed, through:
Establishing whether preconditions for audit are present &
Confirming common understanding between auditor & mgt, TCWG of terms of audit engg.
Preconditions of Audit
Determine whether FRF is acceptable [Factors discussed below]
Obtain agreement of mgt that it acknowledges & understands its responsibility:
i) For preparation of FS as per AFRF
ii) For such Internal Control (IC) to prepare FS free from material misstatement, due to fraud or error &
iii) To provide auditor with:
a) Access to all info. relevant to preparation of FS
b) Additional info. that auditor may request for audit &
c) Unrestricted access to persons within entity to obtain audit evidence.
If preconditions not present, discuss with mgt & do not accept proposed audit.
Factors relevant to auditor's determination of acceptability of FRF to be applied in preparation of FS include:
Nature of entity (business enterprise or not for profit organization)
Purpose of FS (prepared to meet common financial info. needs of wide range of users or specific users)
Nature of FS (complete set of FS or single FS)
Whether law or regulation prescribes AFRF.
Limitation on Scope Prior to Audit Engagement Acceptance
L
If mgt or TCWG impose limitation on scope of audit work such that it'd result in auditor disclaiming opinion
, not accept such audit unless required by law or regulation.
Contents of Agreement on terms of Audit engg. or Engg. Letter (EL)
EL is sent by auditor to his client.
Agreed terms shall be recorded in audit engg. letter or other written agreement & includes:
a) Objective & scope of audit of FS
b) Responsibilities of auditor
c) Responsibilities of mgt
d) Identification of AFRF for preparation of FS
e) Reference to expected form & content of reports to be issued by auditor & a statement
there may be circumstances in which report may differ from its expected form & content.
Acceptance of Change in Terms of Audit Engagement
Auditor shall not agree to change in terms of audit engg. where there is no reasonable justification for doir
✓ If prior to completing audit, auditor is requested to change it to engg. that conveys lower level of assu
determine whether there is reasonable justification for doing so.
✓ If terms are changed, auditor & mgt agree & record new terms of engg. in engg. letter or other written agree
If auditor unable to agree to change of terms & not permitted by mgt to continue:
a. Withdraw from audit engg. where possible under law or regulation &
b. Determine whether obligation to report to other parties, such as TCWG, owners or regulators
Recurring Audits: New engagement letter each time?
Auditor may decide not to send new audit engg. letter or other written agreement each period.
SA 230 "Audit Documentation"
Record of audit procedures, audit evidence & conclusions (commonly known as working papers).
Prepare sufficient audit doc. to enable experienced auditor to understand:
NTE of audit procedures, Results of audit procedures & audit evidence obtained &
Naby& Significant matters arising during audit & conclusions reached, significant professional judgmen-
Extent
In documenting NTE of audit procedures performed, record:
Characteristics of items tested, Who performed audit work & date work was completed &
Who reviewed work performed & date & extent of review.

Following factors make it appropriate to revise terms of audit engg. or remind entity of existing terms:
Entity misunderstands objective & scope of audit.
Recent change of senior mgt.
Significant change in nature or size of business.
Change in FRF adopted in preparation of FS.
Revised or special terms of audit engg.
Significant change in ownership.
Change in legal or regulatory requirements.
Change in other reporting requirements.

Record of audit procedures, audit evidence & conclusions (commonly known as working papers).

Standards on Auditing
Learn
With

SQC 1 "Quality Control for Firms that perform Audits & Reviews of Historical Financial Info &
Other Assurance & Related Services Engagements" (+)
SA 220 Quality Control for an Audit of FS
Firms to have system of quality control that provides reasonable assurance that:
a) Firm & personnel comply with professional standards, regulatory & legal (PRL) requirements, &
b) Reports issued by firm or partners are appropriate in circumstances.
Elements of a System of Quality Control (QC)
Note: Firm should have documented QC P&P & communicate to firm personnel.
Leadership Responsibilities for Quality
Considerations
upholding quality of firm
Following considerations should be taken into account while upholding quality of firm:
Firm's personnel P&P designed to demonstrate its commitment to quality.
)Firm devotes sufficient resources for development & documentation of quality control P&P.
 Firm assigns its mgt responsibilities that commercial considerations don't override quality of work
Before accepting engg acquire vital info about client.
Decide about integrity of Client, promoters & KMP, competence (including capabilities, time &
resources) to perform engg & comply with ethical requirements.

01 Standards on Auc

Ethical Requirements
1. Confidentiality
2. Objectivity
3. Integrity
5. Professional Behaviour
4. Professional Competence & Due Car
Independence Policy
Firm should establish P&P to provide it with reasonable assurance that firm, its personnel & (inclu
experts & network firm) maintain independence.
Policies & procedures should enable firm to:
➤ Communicate independence requirements to personnel & others.
➤ Identify & evaluate circumstances & relationships or breaches creating threat to independence.
➤ Take appropriate action to eliminate threats/reduce to acceptable level/withdrawal from engg.
☐
✓
✓
☑
☑
☑
Notes:
EPs provide relevant info. about client to firm & firm personnel notify firm of threats to independ
All breaches of independence should be promptly notified to firm for appropriate action.
Annually, firm obtains written confirmation of compliance with independence P&P from all personn
✓ Familiarity threat is relevant in FS audits of listed entities, EP rotated after 7 years.
Acceptance & Continuance of Client Relationships & Engg.
Info. such as following assists EP in determining whether decisions regarding acceptance & continu
of audit engg. are appropriate:
Integrity of Client, promoters & key managerial personnel
• Competence (including capabilities, time and resources) to perform engg.
• Compliance with Ethical requirements &
• Significant matters arisen during current or previous audit engg & their implications.
☐
Where issues identified & firm decides to accept/continue client relationship or specific
document how issues were resolved.
Evaluating Integrity of Client
With regard to integrity of a client, matters that firm considers include, for eg:
✓ Identity & business reputation of client's principal owners, key mgt, related parties & TCWG.
☑
☑
☑
☑
✓
✓
Reasons for proposed appointment of firm & non-reappointment of previous firm.
Nature of client's operations, including business practices.
Attitude of client's owners, key mgt & TCWG towards $a/c$ stds & internal control environment.
☑ Whether client is aggressively concerned with maintaining firm's fees as low as possible.
☑
✓ Indications of inappropriate limitation in scope of work.
☑
Indications that client might be involved in money laundering or other criminal activities.

Acceptance & Continuance of Client Relationships & Engg.
Info. such as following assists EP in determining whether decisions regarding acceptance & cor
of audit engg. are appropriate:
Integrity of Client, promoters & key managerial personnel
Competence (including capabilities, time and resources) to perform engg.
Compliance with Ethical requirements &
Significant matters arisen during current or previous audit engg & their implications.
Where issues identified & firm decides to accept/continue client relationship or speci
document how issues were resolved.
Evaluating Integrity of Client
With regard to integrity of a client, matters that firm considers include, for eg:
Identity & business reputation of client's principal owners, key mgt, related parties & TCWG
Reasons for proposed appointment of firm & non-reappointment of previous firm.
Nature of client's operations, including business practices.
Attitude of client's owners, key mgt & TCWG towards $a/c$ stds & internal control environmer
Whether client is aggressively concerned with maintaining firm's fees as low as possible.
Indications of inappropriate limitation in scope of work.
Indications that client might be involved in money laundering or other criminal activities.

Matters to be considered in determining firm's capabilities, competence, time & resources
 Firm personnel have knowledge of relevant industries or subject matters
 Firm personnel have experience with regulatory or reporting requirements or ability to gain necessary
skills & knowledge effectively
 Firm has sufficient personnel with the necessary competence & capabilities
- Experts are available, if needed
- Individuals meeting criteria & eligibility requirements to perform EQCR are available &
-Firm be able to complete engagement within reporting deadline.
Withdrawal from Engg
Policies and procedures on withdrawal from engg/client relationship include:
Discussing with client's mgt & TCWG regarding action that firm might take.
If firm decides to withdraw, discussing with mgt & TCWG withdrawal & reasons for withdrawal.
Considering PRL requirement to report withdrawal, with reasons, to regulatory authorities.
Documenting significant issues, consultations, conclusions and basis for conclusions.

Reviewers should consider whether:
i. Work has been performed as per PRL requirements.
ii. Significant matters raised for further consideration.
iii. Appropriate consultations & resulting conclusions been documented & implemented.
iv. There is need to revise NTE of work performed.
v. Work performed supports the conclusions reached & appropriately documented.
vi. Evidence obtained is sufficient & appropriate to support the report &
vii. Objectives of engg. procedures been achieved.
Engagement Quality Control Review (EQCR)
Engagement quality control reviewer is a
Ο
partner, other person in firm (member of ICAI),
qualified external person, or team of such individuals.
With experience and authority
Ο
Basics:
to objectively evaluate, before report is issued,
significant judgments engg. team made & conclusions reached in formulating report
What EQCR Reviews? Significant judgments made in engg.
• Extent of review depends on complexity of engg. & risk that report might be inappropriate.
Mandatory for all?
Mandatory for audits of FS of listed entities. For others, firm should devise criteria.

. EP shall take responsibility for reviews being performed as per firm's review policies.

Not date auditor's report until completion of EQCR i.e. EQCR should be completed before Audit
Report is issued.
EQCR for audits of FS of listed entities includes considering following aspects (May'24)
- Engg team's evaluation of firm's independence in relation to specific engg.
- Significant risks identified during engg & responses to those risks.
- Judgments made, w.r.t materiality & significant risks.
Whether appropriate consultation taken place on differences of opinion/difficult/contentious matters,
& conclusions from them.
Significance & disposition of corrected & uncorrected misstatements identified during engg.
Matters to be communicated to mgt & TCWG & regulatory bodies.
Whether working papers selected for review reflect work performed in relation to significant
judgments & support conclusions reached.
Appropriateness of report to be issued.
Documentation by EQCR
Procedures required by firm's policies on EQCR been performed.

wordly Sentence
EQCR shall objectively evaluate significant judgments & conclusions reached in preparing audit report
This shall involve:
Discussion of significant matters with EP
Review of FS & proposed auditor's report
Review selected audit documentation relating to significant judgments & conclusions reached
Evaluating conclusions reached in preparing audit report & whether proposed audit report is appropriate 
Firm's policies and procedures are designed to maintain objectivity of EQCR.

Can EP consult EQCR during engagement? Yes, as long as it doesn't affect objectivity of EQCR,

policies & procedures for dealing with & resolving differences of opinion.

Engagement Documentation (ED)
✓ Assembly of final engg. files on timely basis. In case of audit, time limit is not more than 60 days of
Jay date of auditor's report.
Note: Where 2 or more different reports issued for same subject matter of entity, each re
treated as separate engg. for time limit of assembly of audit file.
Eg. when firm issues audit report on a component's financial info. for group consolidation purposes
subsequent date, $A/R$ on same financial info. for statutory purposes.
✓ Retention period ordinarily is no shorter than 7 years from date of auditor's report, or, if later,
of group auditor's report.
Ownership of Engagement Documentation (ED):
ED is property of firm. Firm may make portions/extracts available to clients, provided it do
undermine validity of work performed or for assurance engagements, independence of firm/person
Electronic & Physical Form of Documentation
Ο Establish P&P to maintain confidentiality, safe custody, integrity, accessibility & retrievability of
Ο Where ED is in paper/electronic form integrity, accessibility or retrievability of data me
compromised if documentation can be altered, added to or deleted.
 For practical reasons, original paper documentation may be electronically scanned.
In that case, firm implements procedures requiring engg. teams to:
a) Generate scanned copies of entire content of original paper documentation.
b) Integrate scanned copies into engg files, including indexing & signing off on scanned copies &
c) Enable scanned copies to be retrieved & printed as necessary.
Monitoring
Quality control (QC) of engg. has to be monitored taking into account following factors:
✓ Deciding whether QC system of firm appropriately designed & effectively implemented.
✓ Examining whether new developments in PRL requirements reflected in QC policies.
✓ Conducting monitoring by entrusting responsibility of monitoring process to a partner or other pe
with experience & authority.
☐
✓ Dealing with complaints & allegations against firm or employees of non-compliance with
requirements by a person within or o/s the firm.
☑ Taking appropriate remedial actions against personnel who didn't conform to QC policies.
Taking action when deficiencies in design or operation of firm's QC policies & procedures or
compliance with firm's system of QC identified.
Documentation
Issues identified wrt compliance with relevant ethical requirements & how they were resolved.
Conclusions reached regarding acceptance & continuance of client relationships & audit engg.

Conclusions on compliance with independence requirements & discussions with firm that support these
conclusions.
Nature, scope, & conclusions resulting from consultations undertaken during audit engg.
Peer Review
Examination & review of systems & procedures
TICAL
to determine whether same have been put in place by Practice Unit (PU)
for ensuring quality of assurance services
required by technical, professional & ethical (TPE) Standards or other regulatory requirements.
Peer review Board is constituted by Council of ICAI.
Main objective is to ensure that, in carrying out assurance assignments:
TPE standards including regulatory requirements complied by members of ICAI.
Proper systems in place including documentation which depict quality of assurance services.
On completion of this exercise, "peer review certificate" is issued in case of unqualified report by Peer
Reviewer. In case of qualified report, its informed to PU that same can't be issued with reasons &
Inform about due date for follow-on review decided by Board.
Quality Review Board (QRB)
Set up by CG. It consists of members nominated by CG & ICAI.
Functions of QRB are:
To make recommendations to Council regarding quality of services provided by members of ICAI
To review quality of services provided by members of ICAI including audit services &
To guide members of ICAI to improve quality & comply with statutory & other regulatory requirements
Statutory auditors in respect of companies identified for audit quality review based upon risk-based
approach, Review is carried out by technical reviewers empanelled by QRB.

To guide members of ICAI to improve quality & comply with statutory & other regulatory requirements

approach. Review is carried out by technical reviewers empanelled by QRB.

SA 240: Auditor's Responsibilities relating to Fraud
in an Audit of FS.
Assist auditor in identifying & assessing RoMM due to fraud & procedures to detect such miss
Objectives of auditor
a) To identify & assess RoMM in FS due to fraud
b) To obtain SAAE about assessed RoMM due to fraud, through designing & implementing respo
c) To respond appropriately to identified or suspected fraud.
Misappropriation of assets
Fraudulent Financial Reporting (FFR)
Intentional misstatements in FS to deceive FS Theft of entity's assets & often done by e
users by mgt.
in relatively small & immaterial amounts
Fraudulent financial reporting may be accomplished by:
i. Manipulation, falsification (including forgery), or alteration of $a/c$ records or supporting docum
from which FS are prepared.
ii. Misrepresentation or intentional omission from FS of events, transactions or other significant
iii. Intentional misapplication of $a/c$ principles relating to amts, classification, manner of presente
disclosure.
It often involves mgt override of controls that otherwise may appear to be operating effectiv
Fraud can be committed by mgt overriding controls using such techniques as:
i) Altering records and terms related to Significant and unusual transactions.
ii) Recording Fictitious journal entries, close to end of $a/c$ period, to manipulate operating results
iii) Inappropriately Adjusting Assumptions & changing judgments used to estimate $a/c$ balances.
iv) Concealing, or not disclosing, facts that could affect amounts recorded in F.S.
v) Engaging in Complex transactions to misrepresent financial position or performance of entity.
vi) Omitting, advancing or delaying recognition of events & Transactions in FS occurred during peri

SA 240: Auditor's Responsibilities relating to Fraud
in an Audit of FS.
Assist auditor in identifying & assessing RoMM due to fraud & procedures to detect such misstatem
Objectives of auditor
a) To identify & assess RoMM in FS due to fraud
b) To obtain SAAE about assessed RoMM due to fraud, through designing & implementing responses.
c) To respond appropriately to identified or suspected fraud.
Fraudulent Financial Reporting (FFR)
Misappropriation of assets
Intentional misstatements in FS to deceive FS Theft of entity's assets & often done by emplo
users by mgt.
in relatively small & immaterial amounts
Fraudulent financial reporting may be accomplished by:
i. Manipulation, falsification (including forgery), or alteration of $a/c$ records or supporting document
from which FS are prepared.
ii. Misrepresentation or intentional omission from FS of events, transactions or other significant inf
iii. Intentional misapplication of $a/c$ principles relating to amts, classification, manner of presentatio
disclosure.
It often involves mgt override of controls that otherwise may appear to be operating effectively.
Fraud can be committed by mgt overriding controls using such techniques as:
i) Altering records and terms related to Significant and unusual transactions.
ii) Recording Fictitious journal entries, close to end of $a/c$ period, to manipulate operating results.
iii) Inappropriately Adjusting Assumptions & changing judgments used to estimate $a/c$ balances.
iv) Concealing, or not disclosing, facts that could affect amounts recorded in F.S.
v) Engaging in Complex transactions to misrepresent financial position or performance of entity.
vi) Omitting, advancing or delaying recognition of events & Transactions in FS occurred during period
01 Standards on Auditing
Techniques of Misappropriation of Assets
ESCU
Embezzling receipts (misappropriating collections from debtors)
Learn
wah
Fun
 Stealing physical assets or intellectual property (stealing inventory for personal use/sale)
) Causing entity to pay for goods & services not received (payments to fictitious vendors/employees)
 Using entity's assets for personal use (as collateral for a personal loan or a loan to a related party).
Primary responsibility to prevent & detect fraud → Mgt & TCWG
Auditor Responsibility?
Auditor is responsible for obtaining RA that FS are free from MM due to fraud/error.
Owing to ILA, there is unavoidable risk that some MM may not be detected, even though audit planned
& performed as per SAs. Potential effects of ILA are significant for fraud.
Risk of not detecting a MM from fraud is higher than error. Because fraud may involve sophisticated
& carefully organized schemes designed to conceal it.
Further, risk of not detecting MM from mgt fraud is greater than employee fraud, because mgt is in a
position to directly/indirectly manipulate $a/c$ records, present fraudulent financial info. or override
control procedures to prevent similar frauds by other employees.
When obtaining RA, auditor is responsible for maintaining professional skepticism, considering potential
for mgt override of controls & recognizing that audit procedures effective for detecting error may
not be effective in detecting fraud.
Auditor may accept records & documents as genuine.
If auditor believes document may not be authentic or modified, investigate further.
Where responses to inquiries of mgt or TCWG are inconsistent, investigate the inconsistencies.
What are fraud risk factors?
Events or conditions that indicate incentive or pressure or provide opportunity to commit fraud.
Examples:
Pressure: Need to meet expectations of 3rd parties to obtain additional equity financing
Incentive: Granting of significant bonuses if unrealistic profit targets are met &
• Opportunity: Control environment that is not effective.
Risk factors relating to misstatements arising from fraudulent financial reporting
Incentives/Pressures
Financial stability or profitability is threatened by economic, industry, or operating conditions, such as:
Industry:
R ROH HNS
High degree of competition or market saturation, with declining margins.
Significant declines in customer demand & increasing business failures in industry or overall economy.
Rapid growth or unusual profitability compared to other companies in the same industry.

personal loan or a loan to a related party).

Opportunities
Nature of industry or operations provides opportunities to engage in FFR that can arise from followi
Strong financial presence or ability to dominate a certain industry that allows entity to dictate T&
to suppliers or customers that may result in inappropriate or non-arm's-length transactions.
RPTs/Intermediaries/Complex transactions
Significant related-party transactions not in ordinary course of business or with related entities n
audited or audited by another firm.
Use of business intermediaries with no clear business justification.
Significant, unusual, or highly complex transactions, close to period end that pose difficult "substan
over form" questions.

Monitoring of mgt is not effective due to: -
Domination of mgt by single person/small group without compensating controls.
Oversight by TCWG over financial reporting process & internal control not effective.
There is a complex or unstable organizational structure, as evidenced by:
Difficulty in determining organization or individuals that have controlling interest in entity.
Overly complex org. structure involving unusual legal entities or managerial lines of authority.
High turnover of senior mgt, legal counsel, or TCWG.
Internal control components are deficient due to:
- Inadequate monitoring of controls, including automated controls & controls over interim financial
reporting (where external reporting is required).

 Accounting & info systems that are not effective, including situations involving significant deficiencies
in internal control.

01 Standards on Aud
Relationship b/w mgt and current or predecessor auditor is strained, as exhibited by:
Domineering mgt behaviour, influencing scope of audit work or selection or continuance of auditor
Unreasonable demands on auditor, such as unrealistic time constraints for audit completion.
Frequent disputes with current or predecessor auditor on accounting, auditing, or reporting matte
Restrictions on auditor that inappropriately limit access to people or info or TCWG.
Risk Factors Relating to Misstatements Arising from Misappropriation of Assets
1. Incentives/Pressures: Personal financial obligations may create pressure on mgt or employees
access to cash or other assets. Adverse relationships between entity & employees with access to
or other assets susceptible to theft.
PKR
For eg, adverse relationships may be created by:
✓ Known or anticipated future employee layoffs.
Recent or anticipated changes to employee compensation or benefit plans.
✓ Promotions, compensation or other rewards inconsistent with expectations.
2. Opportunities: Certain circumstances increase susceptibility of assets to misappropriation.
For eg, opportunities to misappropriate assets increase in case of following:
FLIC
Rods
✓ Large amounts of cash on hand
✓ Inventory items small in size, of high value, or in high demand.
Fixed assets  small in size, marketable, or lacking observable identification of ownership.
✓ Easily convertible assets, such as bearer bonds, diamonds, or computer chips.
Inadequate IC over assets may increase susceptibility of misappropriation of those assets
For eg, misappropriation of assets may occur because of: doper sod
 Lack of documentation of transactions, for eg, credits for merchandise returns.
•
Inadequate oversight of senior mgt expenditures, eg travel and other reimbursements.
0 Inadequate physical safeguards over cash, investments, inventory, or fixed assets.
• Inadequate record keeping with respect to assets.
• Inadequate Segregation of Duties or independent checks.
3. Attitudes/Rationalizations
✓ Disregard for need for monitoring or reducing risks related to misappropriations of assets.
Disregard for internal control over misappropriation of assets.
Behavior indicating displeasure or dissatisfaction with entity or its treatment of employee.
✓ Changes in behavior or lifestyle that may indicate assets have been misappropriated.
✓ Tolerance of petty theft.

 Standards on Auditing
Learn
with
Fun
Why evaluation of fraud risk factors by auditor necessary?
Fraud risk factors may not necessarily indicate existence of fraud but may indicate RoMM due to fraud.
Discussion among Engagement Team
Include how & where FS may be susceptible to material misstatement due to fraud, including how fraud
might occur. Discussion shall occur even if engg. team members' believe mgt & TCWG are honest.
RAP & Related Activities for Obtaining Info. for Identifying RoMM due to Fraud
For identifying RoMM due to fraud, make inquiries of mgt & others within entity, obtaining understanding
as to how TCWG exercise oversight of mgt's processes for identifying & responding to risks of fraud in
entity & internal control to mitigate these risks & evaluation of unexpected relationships identified in
analytical procedures.
Auditor shall:
Responses to assessed RoMM due to fraud at FS level
Assign & supervise personnel considering their knowledge, skill & ability of individuals to be given
significant engg responsibilities
 Evaluate selection & application of $a/c$ policies by entity, related to subjective measurements & complex
transactions, whether it's fraudulent financial reporting mgt's effort to manage earnings
Incorporate element of unpredictability in selection of NTE of audit procedures.
Further Audit procedures responsive to assessed RoMM due to fraud at assertion level
Auditor may change NTE of audit procedures to obtain audit evidence that's more reliable & relevant
or obtain additional corroborative info.
For eg, if auditor identifies mgt is under pressure to meet earnings expectations, risk that mgt is
inflating sales by entering sales agreements that include terms that preclude revenue recognition or
by invoicing sales before delivery.
✓ Auditor may design external confirmations not only to confirm $o/s$ amounts, but also confirm details
of sales agreements, including date, rights of return & delivery terms.
✓ In addition, auditor might make inquiries of non-financial personnel regarding changes in sales
agreements & delivery terms.
Audit Procedures Responsive to Risks Related to Mgt Override of Controls
Mgt can perpetrate fraud because of its ability to manipulate $a/c$ records & prepare fraudulent FS by
overriding controls that appear to be operating effectively.
It's a ROMM due to fraud & thus a significant risk.
Design & perform audit procedures to:
Test appropriateness of journal entries recorded in general ledger & other adjustments in FS.
Review
$a/c$ estimates for biases & evaluate circumstances represent ROMM due to fraud.

Considering Whether an Identified Misstatement may be Indicative of Fraud
If there is indication → consider implications of misstatement in relation to other aspects
particularly reliability of management representations (SA 580).
Evaluation of Audit Evidence
Evaluate whether analytical procedures performed when forming overall conclusion indicate pre
unrecognized RoMM due to fraud.
When auditor identifies a misstatement, evaluate whether it's fraud. If yes, evaluate implicat
other aspects, particularly reliability of WR, recognizing that it is unlikely to be isolated occurr
✓ If auditor identifies a misstatement (material or not) & believes that it $is/may$ be result of fr
mgt (particularly, senior mgt) is involved, re-assess RoMM due to fraud & NTE of audit procedur
✓ Also consider if there's possible collusion involving employees, mgt or $3^{rd}$ parties when reconsic
reliability of evidence previously obtained.

implications for audit.

Auditor Unable to Complete Engagement
If auditor concludes it isn't possible to continue performing audit as result of misstatement resu
from fraud or suspected fraud, auditor should:
i. Determine professional & legal responsibilities, to report to person(s) who made audit appointmer
regulatory authorities
Fi. Consider possibility of withdrawing from engg. &
i. If auditor withdraws:
✓ discuss with mgt & TCWG, withdrawal from engg. & reasons &
✓ consider professional or legal requirement to report to person(s) who made audit appointmen
regulatory authorities, withdrawal & reasons.

results of mgt's assessment of risk that FS may be materially misstated as a result of fraud

Poles in internal control or where fraud could have a material effect on FS &
nowledge of allegations of fraud, or suspected fraud, affecting entity's FS communicated
mployees, former employees, analysts, regulators or others.

 Standards on Auditing
Communications to Mgt & TCWG
Learn
With
Fun
If auditor identifies a fraud, communicate on a timely basis to appt. level of mgt.
Unless all TCWG involved in managing entity, if auditor identified or suspects fraud involving mgt,
employees who have significant roles in internal control or others where fraud results in a material
misstatement in FS, communicate these matters to TCWG on timely basis.
If auditor suspects fraud involving mgt, communicate these suspicions to TCWG & discuss with them
NTE of audit procedures to complete the audit.
Auditor shall communicate with TCWG any other relevant matters related to fraud.
Documentation of understanding entity & its environment & assessment of RoMM shall include: -
Significant decisions of engg.team regarding susceptibility of entity's FS to MM due to fraud &
Identified & assessed RoMM due to fraud at FS level & assertion level.
Documentation of responses to assessed RoMM include: -
Overall responses to assessed RoMM due to fraud at FS level & NTE of audit procedures &
Results of audit procedures, including those designed to address risk of mgt override of controls.
Communications about fraud made to mgt, TCWG, regulators & others.
Presumption of Fraud Risk in Revenue Recognition
Auditor based on presumption that there are risks of fraud in revenue recognition, evaluate which types
of revenue, revenue transactions or assertions give rise to such risks.
Fraud risk may be rebutted i.e.
No RoMM due to fraud
High probability of fraud risk
Complex Revenue Structure OR
Single type of simple revenue structure.
Lack of controls
Eg. Leasehold revenue from single rental
property

document reasons for that conclusion.

 Standards on Aude
SA 250: Consideration of Laws & Regulations in an Audit of FS
Assist auditer in identifying MM in Fs due to Non-compliance with laws & regulations (NOCLAR).
Objectives of auditor
a) Te ebtain SAAE regarding compliance with those L&Rs having direct effect on determining ma
ameunts & disclosures in FS
b) To perferm specified audit procedures to identify instances of non-compliance with other lo
regulations that may have a material effect on FS &
 ) To respond appropriately te non-compliance or suspected non-compliance with laws & regula
identified during audit.
Mgt Responsibility for compliance with L&Rs
Following are procedures entity implement to assist in prevention & detection of NOCLAR:
a) Menitering legal requirements & ensuring operating procedures designed to meet these requiremen
b) Instituting & operating systems of internal control.
c) Developing, publicising & following a code of conduct.
d) Ensuring employees are trained & understand code of conduct.
e) Monitoring compliance with code of conduct & discipline employees who fail to comply with it.
f) Engaging legal advisors to assist in monitoring legal requirements.
 ) Maintaining register of significant applicable L&Rs & record of complaints.
$(L/Rs)$
Auditor's responsibility in relation to Compliance with Laws & Regulations
Auditor responsible for obtaining reasonable assurance that FS are free from material misstatem
due to fraud/error. Auditor considers applicable legal & regulatory framework.
Owing to inherent limitations of audit, there is unavoidable risk that some MM may not be detec
even though audit planned & performed as per SAs
In context of L/Rs, potential effects of inherent limitations on auditor's ability to detect MM
greater for such reasons as following! =
a) There are many laws & regulations, relating to operating aspects of entity that typically don't af
FS & not captured by entity's info, systems relevant to financial reporting.
b) Non-compliance may involve conduct designed to conceal it, such as collusion, forgery, etc.
c) Whether act is a non-compliance is ultimately matter for legal determination by court of law.
2 Types of $L/Rs$
Auditor's responsibilities in relation to compliance with 2 different categories of L&Rs:
1. Provisions of those L&Rs having direct effect on determination of material amounts & disclosures
such as tox & labour laws &

01 Standards on Auditing
Learn
wah
Fun
Other L&Rs that don't have direct effect on determination of amounts & disclosures in FS. but
compliance with which may be fundamental to operating aspects of business.
For 1st category, obtain SAAE about compliance with L&Rs.
For 2nd category, auditor's responsibility is limited to undertake specified audit procedures to identify
non-compliance with those L/Rs that may have material effect on FS.
Auditor's Consideration of Compliance with Laws and Regulations
Obtain general understanding of:
a) legal & regulatory framework applicable to entity & industry &
b) How entity is complying with that framework.
_ Obtain SAAE regarding compliance with those L&Rs that have direct effect on determination of
material amounts & disclosures in FS
•
Perform following audit procedures to identify instances of non-compliance with other L&Rs that
may have a material effect on FS:
a) Inquiring of mgt &
b) Inspecting correspondence, if any, with relevant licensing or regulatory authorities.
Remain alert that other audit procedures may bring instances of non-compliance.
Obtain written representation that known instances of NOCLAR have been disclosed to auditor.
Understanding → SAAE (direct) → I/I (NOCLAR) → Alert! → WR
Audit Procedures When Non-compliance is Identified or Suspected
If auditor becomes aware of instance of NOCLAR, obtain:
Understanding of nature of activity & circumstances in which it has occurred &
Further info. to evaluate possible effect on FS
If auditor suspects there may be non-compliance, discuss with mgt & TCWG.
• If mgt or TCWG don't provide sufficient info. & impact maybe material, obtain legal advice.
If sufficient info can't be obtained, evaluate effect of lack of SAAE on auditor's opinion.
Evaluate effect of non-compliance on other aspects of audit,
✓
including auditor's risk assessment &
✓
reliability of written representations, & take appropriate action.
Reporting of Identified or Suspected Non-Compliance with L/R (NOCLAR)
Reporting NOLCAR to TCWG:
Communicate with TCWG NOCLAR coming to auditor's attention, other than inconsequential matters.
If, NOCLAR is intentional & material, communicate matter to TCWG as soon as practicable.
If auditor suspects mgt or TCWG involved in noncompliance, communicate matter to next higher level
of authority at entity, such as audit committee or supervisory board. Where no higher authority exists,
or auditor believes communication may not be acted upon, obtain legal advice.
1.17

Further info. to evaluate possible effect on FS
• If auditor suspects there may be non-compliance, discuss with mgt & TCWG.
If mgt or TCWG don't provide sufficient info. & impact maybe material, obtain legal advice.
If sufficient info can't be obtained, evaluate effect of lack of SAAE on auditor's opinion.
Evaluate effect of non-compliance on other aspects of audit,
✓
including auditor's risk assessment &
✓
reliability of written representations, & take appropriate action.
Reporting of Identified or Suspected Non-Compliance with L/R (NOCLAR)
Reporting NOLCAR to TCWG:
Communicate with TCWG NOCLAR coming to auditor's attention, other than inconsequential matters.
If, NOCLAR is intentional & material, communicate matter to TCWG as soon as practicable.
If auditor suspects mgt or TCWG involved in noncompliance, communicate matter to next higher level
of authority at entity, such as audit committee or supervisory board. Where no higher authority exists,
or auditor believes communication may not be acted upon, obtain legal advice.

With
Fun

rather than mgt or TCWG, evaluate effect on auditor's opinion as per SA 705.
a.

NOCLAR, determine whether he has responsibility to report to parties outside the entity.

01 Standards on Auditing
SA 260 "Communication with Those Charged With Governance"
Significance of communication
ffective 2-way communication is important in assisting:
Learn
wah
Fun
uditor & TCWG in understanding matters related to audit & developing a constructive working
elationship.
uditor in obtaining info. relevant to audit.
or eg, TCWG may assist auditor in understanding entity & its environment, identifying appropriate
ources of audit evidence & providing info about specific transactions or events.
CWG in fulfilling responsibility to oversee financial reporting process (FRP), reducing ROMM of FS.

o communicate clearly with TCWG responsibilities of auditor and planned scope & timing of audit.
o obtain from TCWG info relevant to audit.
o provide TCWG with timely observations significant & relevant in overseeing FRP.
o promote effective two-way communication between auditor & TCWG
ote:
etermine appropriate person(s) within entity's governance structure with whom to communicate.

uditor's responsibilities in relation to FS Audit (Included in engg letter)
 ) Auditor is responsible for forming & expressing opinion on FS &

01 Standards on Auditing
Auditor Independence (Listed Entities)
In case of listed entities, auditor shall communicate with TCWG in writing:
Learn
wah
Fun
) Statement that engg. team & others in firm have complied with ethical requirements of independence,
) All relationships & other matters between firm, network firms, and entity that bear on independence.
This shall include total fees charged during period covered by FS,
✓ for audit & non-audit services provided by firm & network firms
✓ to entity & components controlled by entity.
 Related safeguards to eliminate identified threats to independence or reduce them to acceptable level.
Documentation
Where matters communicated orally, include them in audit documentation, & when & to whom they were
communicated. Where communicated in writing, retain a copy of communication.
Communication of Key Audit Matters [Timing & Usefulness]
SA 260 requires auditor to communicate with TCWG on a timely basis.
SA 701 states that appropriate timing for communications about KAM will vary with circumstances of
engg. However, auditor may communicate preliminary views about KAM when discussing planned scope
& timing of audit, & further discuss when communicating about audit findings.
Doing so may help to alleviate practical challenges to have a robust 2 - way dialogue about KAM at time
the FS are finalized for issuance.
Communication with TCWG enables them to be aware of KAM & provides them opportunity to obtain
further clarification if necessary. Auditor may provide TCWG with draft of auditor's report to
facilitate this discussion.
Communication with TCWG recognizes their important role in overseeing financial reporting process, &
understand basis for auditor's decisions in relation to KAM & how these matters will be described in
uditor's report.
Iso enables TCWG to consider whether new or enhanced disclosures may be useful in light of fact
That these matters will be communicated in auditor's report.

SA 265 "Communicating Deficiencies in Internal Control to TCWG & Mgt"
Auditor's Objective
To communicate to TCWG & mgt, deficiencies in IC that auditor identified & in auditor's pro
judgment of sufficient importance to merit their respective attentions.
Deficiency in IC:
ⅰ) Control designed, implemented or operated in a way it's unable to Prevent/Detect/ 
misstatements in FS on a timely basis or
ii) A control necessary to $P/D/C$ misstatements in FS on a timely basis is missing.
Significant deficiency in IC: Deficiency or combination of deficiencies in IC, in auditor's prof=
judgment, of sufficient importance to merit attention of TCWG.
Note: Significance of a deficiency(s) in internal control depends not only on whether a missta
has actually occurred, but also on likelihood & potential magnitude of misstatement.
Significant deficiencies may exist even though auditor has not identified misstatements during
Requirements
➤ Determine whether, on basis of work performed, he has identified one or more deficiencies in
If identified one or more deficiencies in IC, determine, on basis of work performed, whether, inc
or in combination, they constitute significant deficiencies.
Auditor shall communicate in writing significant deficiencies in IC to TCWG on a timely basis.
➤ Auditor shall also communicate to mgt at an appropriate level of responsibility on a timely basis
a. In writing, significant deficiencies in IC that auditor has communicated or intends to com
to TCWG, unless it would be inappropriate to communicate directly to mgt in circumstances
b. Other deficiencies in IC that haven't been communicated to mgt by other parties & are of s
importance to merit mgt's attention.
➤ Written communication to TCWG
Auditor shall include in written communication of significant deficiencies in internal control:
a. Description of deficiencies & explanation of their potential effects &
5. Sufficient info. to enable TCWG & mgt understand context of communication.
In particular, auditor shall explain that:
Purpose of audit was to express opinion on FS
Audit included consideration of Internal Controls relevant to preparation of FS to des
procedures, but not for purpose of expressing opinion on effectiveness of IC &
Matters reported are limited to deficiencies that auditor identified & concluded are of 5
importance to merit being reported to TCWG

 Standards on Auditing
How to determine if there are significant deficiencies in Internal Control? (Inter)
Learn
With
Fun
Indicators of significant deficiencies in internal control include, for eg:
Evidence of ineffective entity risk assessment process (RAP), such as mgt's failure to identify ROMM.
Misstatements detected by audit procedures not prevented/detected/corrected, by entity's IC.
Disclosure of material misstatement due to error or fraud as prior period items in CY's P&L.
Evidence of mat's inability to oversee preparation of FS.
Evidence of ineffective response to identified significant risks (eg, absence of controls over such risk).
Evidence of ineffective control environment:
a) Identification of mgt fraud, whether or not material, not prevented by entity's IC.
b) Mgt's failure to implement remedial action on significant deficiencies previously communicated.
c) Absence of a risk assessment process (RAP) within entity.
4
Ineffective RAP >> Misstatement detected >> Prior period item >> Inablity to overseep prep of FS >>
Ineffective response + Control environment
How to decide if Identified Deficiency is Significant or Not? (Inter) FLIVS
Eg. of matters that auditor may consider in determining whether a deficiency or combination of
DaRaction
deficiencies in internal control constitutes a significant deficiency include:
Likelihood of deficiencies leading to MM in F.S. in future.
Interaction of deficiency with other deficiencies in internal control.
Volume of activity that has occurred in account balance or class of transactions exposed to deficiency.
FS amounts exposed to deficiencies.
Susceptibility to loss or fraud of related asset or liability.
Importance of controls to financial reporting process; for example:
 General monitoring controls (such as oversight of management).
 Controls over the prevention and detection of fraud.
➤ Controls over the selection and application of significant accounting policies.
➤ Controls over significant transactions with related parties.
 Controls over significant transactions $o/s$ entity's normal course of business.
 Controls over the period-end financial reporting process (such as controls over non-recurring journal
entries).
Likelihood >> Interaction  Volume of Activity >> F.S. Amts expose >> Loss/fraud » Imp of controls
When to communicate significant deficiencies?
Listed entities: before approval of FS
Other entities: before assembly of audit file

 Standards on Auditin
Fun
SA 299 "Joint Audit of FS"
Joint audit is audit of FS by 2 or more auditors. Such auditors are called joint auditors.
For eg, Sec 139(3) of Companies Act, 2013 provides that members of a Co. may resolve to provide the
audit shall be conducted by more than one auditor.
Audit Planning, Risk Assessment and Allocation of Work
• EP & other key members of team from each of joint auditors involved in planning.
Joint auditors jointly establish overall audit strategy that sets scope, timing & direction of audit
guides development of audit plan.
• Prior to commencement of audit, joint auditors discuss & develop a joint audit plan.
In developing joint audit plan, joint auditors shall: fractas,
a) Identify division of audit areas & common audit areas that define scope of work of each joint auditor
Joint auditors should, divide audit work. Usually in terms of audit of identifiable units or specified are
In some cases, due to nature of business, such division may not be possible.
In such cases, division maybe w.r.t items of assets/liabilities/income/expenditure.
Certain areas of work, may not be divided & covered by all joint auditors.
b) Ascertain reporting objectives of engagement to plan timing of audit & nature of communications
c) Communicate among all joint auditors factors significant in directing engagement team's efforts
d) Consider results of preliminary engagement activities & knowledge gained on other engagements
e) Ascertain NTE of resources necessary to perform engagement
✓ At this stage, RoMM assessed by each joint auditor & communicated to other joint auditors
documented, whether pertaining to overall FS level or area of allocation among other joint auditors
✓ Joint auditors discuss & document NTE of audit procedures for common & specific allotted areas
communicate to TCWG.
☑ Joint auditors shall obtain common Engg Letter & common mgt representation letter.
✓ Work allocation document shall be signed by all joint auditors & communicated to TCWG.
Responsibility and Co-ordination among Joint Auditors
 In respect of audit work divided, each joint auditor responsible only for work allocated.
All the joint auditors shall be jointly & severally responsible for:
a. audit work not divided among joint auditors & carried by all joint auditors
b. decisions taken by all joint auditors under audit planning for common areas concerning NTE
procedures to be performed by each Joint Auditor [Execution  Individual Responsibility]
c. matters brought to notice by any one of them & on which there is agreement among all
d. examining that FS comply with relevant statutes
e. Presentation & disclosure of FS as required by AFRF
f. Ensuring Audit Report complies with relevant statutes, SAs & ICAI pronouncements

01 Standards on Auditing
Learn
Fun
Where joint auditor comes across matters relevant to others & deserve their attention, communicate to
thers in writing prior to completion of audit.
esponsibility of each joint auditor to determine NTE of audit procedures to be applied in relation to
reas of work allocated.
Individual responsibility of each joint auditor to study & evaluate system of internal control &
ssessment of risk relating to areas of work allocated to said joint auditor.
Audit Conclusion & Reporting
Toint auditors required to issue common audit report, however, where there's disagreement w.r.t opinion
or any matters of audit report, express opinion in a separate audit report.
 joint auditor is not bound by views of majority & shall express opinion in separate audit report in case
of disagreement.
In such circumstances, audit report(s)
issued by joint auditor(s) shall make reference to separate audit
-eport(s) issued by other joint auditor(s).
Further, separate audit report shall also make reference to report issued by other joint auditor.
Such reference shall be made under heading OM Para as per SA 706.
Each joint auditor entitled to assume that:
Others have carried out their part of audit work as per SAs.
It isn't necessary for joint auditor to review work performed by other joint auditors or perform any
Tests in order to ascertain whether work actually been performed in such manner.
Other joint auditors have brought to said joint auditor's notice any departure from AFRF or significant
observations noticed in audit.
Division/Branch: Where FS of a division/branch audited by one of joint auditors, other joint auditors
entitled to assume that such FS comply with legal & regulatory requirements & present true & fair view
Before finalizing their audit report, joint auditors discuss & communicate their respective conclusion 
That would form content of audit report.
Communication with TCWG
"Performing well in Exams is JOINT effort of Teacher & Student,
So do REVISE well, so that our efforts pay off well"
01 Standards on Auditing
Learn
Fun
Where joint auditor comes across matters relevant to others & deserve their attention, communicate to
others in writing prior to completion of audit.
Responsibility of each joint auditor to determine NTE of audit procedures to be applied in relation to
areas of work allocated.
Individual responsibility of each joint auditor to study & evaluate system of internal control &
assessment of risk relating to areas of work allocated to said joint auditor.
Audit Conclusion & Reporting
Joint auditors required to issue common audit report, however, where there's disagreement w.r.t opinion
or any matters of audit report, express opinion in a separate audit report.
A joint auditor is not bound by views of majority & shall express opinion in separate audit report in case
of disagreement.
issued by joint auditor(s) shall make reference to separate audit
In such circumstances, audit report(s)
report(s) issued by other joint auditor(s).
Further, separate audit report shall also make reference to report issued by other joint auditor.
Such reference shall be made under heading OM Para as per SA 706.
Each joint auditor entitled to assume that:
Others have carried out their part of audit work as per SAs.
It isn't necessary for joint auditor to review work performed by other joint auditors or perform any
tests in order to ascertain whether work actually been performed in such manner.
Other joint auditors have brought to said joint auditor's notice any departure from AFRF or significan-
observations noticed in audit.
Division/Branch: Where FS of a division/branch audited by one of joint auditors, other joint auditor
entitled to assume that such FS comply with legal & regulatory requirements & present true & fair view
Before finalizing their audit report, joint auditors discuss & communicate their respective conclusion
that would form content of audit report.
Communication with TCWG
"Performing well in Exams is JOINT effort of Teacher & Student,
So do REVISE well, so that our efforts pay off well"

01 Standards on Auditin
SA 315: Identifying & Assessing ROMM through understanding Entity and its Environment
Objective of Auditor (Inter)
Identify & assess ROMM, due to fraud or error at FS & assertion levels,
through understanding entity & its environment, including entity's internal control,
providing basis for designing & implementing responses to assessed ROMM.
What methods included in Auditor's Risk assessment procedures (RAP)
Audit procedures performed to obtain understanding of entity & its environment, including interna
control, to identify & assess ROMM, due to fraud or error, at FS & assertion levels.
Risk assessment procedures shall include following:
a. Inquiries of mgt & others within entity to identify ROMM due to fraud or error.
b. Analytical procedures.
C. Observation and inspection.
Auditor shall obtain an understanding of following: [Knowledge of Business] (Inter)
1. Relevant industry, regulatory, and other external factors including applicable FRF.
. Nature of entity, including:
i) its operations
ii) its ownership and governance structures
iii) types of investments &
iv) way entity is structured & financed; to enable auditor to understand ABCD to be expected in FS.
Entity's selection & application of $A/C$ policies, including reasons for changes thereto.
Entity's objectives & strategies & related business risks that may result in ROMM.
Measurement & review of entity's financial performance.
Auditor's Process of Assessing the RoMM (Inter)
Identify risks through understanding of entity & its environment, including controls related to risks
Assess identified risks & evaluate if they relate pervasively to FS affecting many assertions
Relate identified risks to what can go wrong at assertion level, considering relevant controls audito
intends to test &
Consider likelihood of misstatement(s) & whether it's magnitude could result in material misstatemen
Significant Risks (Inter)
Identified & assessed RoMM that, in auditor's judgment, requires special audit consideration.
Examples of Significant Risk?
Risk of fraud.
Significant economic, accounting, or other developments like changes in regulatory environment, etc
Complexity of transactions

SA 330: Auditor's Responses to Assessed Risks
Further Audit Procedures in response to assessed ROMM at assertion level
In designing further audit procedures to be performed, the auditor shall:
Consider reasons for assessment of RoMM at assertion level for each ABCD, including:
i. Likelihood of material misstatement due to characteristics of relevant ABCD (i.e., inherent risk) &
ii. Whether risk assessment takes into account relevant controls (i.e., control risk), thereby requiring
auditor to obtain audit evidence to determine whether controls are operating effectively
(i.e., auditor intends to rely on operating effectiveness of controls in determining NTE of substantive
procedures) &
 Obtain more persuasive audit evidence higher auditor's assessment of risk.
Further Audit Procedures comprise of:
Tests of controls, when required by SAs or when auditor chosen to do so
Substantive procedures, including tests of details & substantive analytical procedures.
Test of controls: Audit procedure to evaluate operating effectiveness of controls, in preventing, or
detecting & correcting (P/D/C), material misstatements at assertion level.
Auditor shall design & perform TOCs when:
Auditor's assessment of RoMM at assertion level includes expectation that controls are operating
effectively (i.e., auditor intends to rely on operating effectiveness of controls in determining NTE o
substantive procedures) OR
Substantive procedures alone can't provide SAAE at assertion level.
If auditor plans to use audit evidence from previous audit, establish continuing relevance by obtainin
audit evidence about whether significant changes have occurred in controls.
Obtain this evidence by performing inquiry combined with observation or inspection, confirr
understanding of those specific controls, and:
If there have been changes that affect continuing relevance of audit evidence from previous audit, sha
Test controls in current audit.
If there have not been such changes, auditor shall test controls at least once in every third audit.
Controls over Significant Risks to be tested in current period.

01 Standards on Audit
Using audit evidence obtained during an interim period
When auditor obtains audit evidence about operating effectiveness of controls during interim perio
a. Obtain audit evidence about significant changes to those controls subsequent to interim period &
b. Determine the additional audit evidence to be obtained for the remaining period.
Substantive procedure: Audit procedure to detect Material misstatement at assertion level.
Substantive procedures comprise:
i) Tests of details (of classes of transactions, account balances, and disclosures), &
ii) Substantive analytical procedures.
Substantive Procedure compulsory?
Irrespective of assessed RoMM, design and perform substantive procedures for each material ABC
This reflects that:
i) auditor's risk assessment is judgmental & may not identify all RoMM &
ii) there are inherent limitations to internal control, including management override.
SA 320: Materiality in Planning & Performing an Audit
Determining Materiality & Performance Materiality when planning Audit
When establishing overall audit strategy, determine materiality for FS as whole.
 If, there's one or more particular ABCD for which misstatements of lesser amounts than material
for FS as whole could influence economic decisions of users taken on basis of FS, also determ
materiality level(s) for those particular ABCD.
 Determine performance materiality for assessing ROMM & determining NTE of FAP.
Performance Materiality (PM) means
amount or amounts set by auditor at less than materiality for F.S. as whole
to reduce probability that aggregate of uncorrected & undetected misstatements
exceeds materiality for FS as a whole.
If applicable, PM also refers to amt or amounts set by auditor at less than materiality level or level
particular ABCD.
Benchmark
Determining materiality involves exercise of professional judgment. A percentage is applied to cho
benchmark as starting point in determining materiality for FS as whole.
VEOLA
Factors that affect identification of appropriate benchmark include following:
Elements of FS (for eg, assets, liabilities, equity, revenue, expenses)
01 Standards on Auditing
Learn
With
Fun
Whether there are items on which attention of users of particular entity's FS tends to be focused (for
eg, for evaluating financial performance users may focus on profit, revenue or net assets)
Nature of entity, where entity is in its life cycle, industry & economic environment
Entity's ownership structure & way it is financed (for eg, if entity is financed solely by debt rather thar
equity, users may put more emphasis on assets, & claims on them, than on entity's earnings) &
Relative volatility of benchmark.
Element of FS >> Focus >> Ownership structure >>> Life cycle >>> Volatility
Note: There is relationship between %age & chosen benchmark, such that a percentage applied to PB
from continuing operations will normally be higher than %age applied to total revenue.
Examples of benchmarks include categories of reported income such as profit before tax, total revenue
gross profit, total expenses, total equity or net asset value.
benchmark
Revision of Materiality
Revise materiality for FS as a whole (& if applicable, materiality level for particular ABCD) in event of
becoming aware of info.* during audit that would have caused auditor to have determined different
amount initially.
* may need to be revised as a result of
change in circumstances during audit (for eg, decision to dispose of major part of business),
new information, or
change in auditor's understanding of entity & its operations as a result of FAP.
For eg, if actual financial results are substantially different from anticipated period end financial
results that were used initially to determine materiality for FS, auditor revises that materiality.
If auditor concludes lower materiality for FS as whole (&, if applicable, materiality level(s) for
particular ABCD) than initially determined, determine whether necessary to revise Performance
Materiality & whether NTE of FAP remain appropriate.

SA 402: Audit Considerations relating to an Entity
Using a Service Organisation (SO)
Payroll Accounting
ITC Ltd
User
Entity
X
Housekeeping
Jetha Service Providers
Service
Organisation
Tappu Service Providers
Sub-Service
Organisation
 User entity: Entity that uses a service organisation & whose FS are being audited.
 User auditor is auditor who audits & reports on FS of user entity.
 Service organisation (SO) is a $3^{rd}$ party org. that provides services to user entities that are part of
entity's information systems relevant to financial reporting.
Service auditor is auditor who, at request of SO, provides assurance report on controls of a SO.
Note:
Services provided by SO are relevant to audit of a user entity's F.S. when those services, & contr
over them, are part of user entity's information system, including related business processes, relever
to financial reporting.
How to know if SO's services are relevant to user entity's Financial Reporting?
SOs services are part of user entity's info. system, relevant to financial reporting if these service!
affect any of following:
SCoTs: Classes of transactions in entity's operations that are significant to FS
Procedures: Procedures, within both IT & manual systems, by which those transactions are initiate
recorded, processed, corrected, trfd to general ledger & reported in FS.
Records: Related a/c records, used to initiate, record, process & report transaction.
Info system: How info. system captures events & conditions, other than transactions, significant to fs
RP: Financial reporting process to prepare FS
nusual Transactions: Controls surrounding journal entries, including non-standard JEs used to recor
on-recurring, unusual transactions or adjustments.
$SCoTs\gg Procedures+A/c~records\gg NSCoTs\gg FRP+JEs$
Auditor's Objective
4Norsionificant chers of ioms
understand services provided by SO & effect on user entity's internal control &
design & perform audit procedures responsive to those risks.

 Standards on Auditing
Types of Reports
Type 1 Report: Description & design of ICs at Specified date
Type 2 Report: Description, design & Operating effectiveness of ICs throughout period
Learn
With
Fun
[Covered in Detail in SAE 3402
Obtaining understanding of services provided by Service Organisation (SO) NNDN
When understanding entity as per SA 315, obtain understanding of how entity uses services of SO
Nature of services provided by SO & significance of those services to user entity, including effect o
user entity's IC. Info can be taken from User manuals, Contract, report by Service Auditor etc
Nature & materiality of transactions processed by SO
Degree of interaction between activities of SO & user entity i.e. extent to which user entity ha
mplemented effective controls over processes performed by SO.
Eg. High degree of interaction if user entity authorise transactions processed by SO.
Nature of relationship between user entity & SO, including contractual terms for activities by SO.
Nature & Significance/Materiality >> Interaction + Relationship
Auditor's Considerations
Evaluate design & implementation of controls at user entity that relate to services provided by SO.
Determine whether sufficient understanding of nature & significance of services provided by SO & the
effect on user entity's IC been obtained for identification & assessment of ROMM.
If auditor unable to obtain sufficient understanding from user entity, perform following procedures:
Obtaining a Type 1 or Type 2 report, if available
Contacting SO, through user entity, to obtain specific info
Visiting SO & performing procedures that will provide info about controls at SO
Using another auditor to perform procedures that provide necessary info about controls at SO
Using Type 1 Or 2 Report to Support User Auditor's Understanding of SO:
In determining SAAE provided by Type 1 or 2 report, user auditor shall be satisfied as to:
Service auditor's professional competence (except where service auditor is member of ICAI)
Independence from SO &
Adequacy of standards under which Type 1 or 2 report was issued.
If user auditor plans to use Type $1/2$ report as audit evidence to support user auditor's understandin
about design & implementation of controls at SO, user auditor shall:
Evaluate whether description & design of controls at SO is at a date or for a appropriate period
Evaluate sufficiency & appropriateness of evidence provided by report for understanding user entity
IC relevant to audit &

01 Standards on Audi
c. Determine whether complementary user entity controls identified by So relevant to user entity &
obtain understanding of whether user entity has designed & implemented such controls
Complementary user entity controls refer to controls that SO assumes user entity will impleme
which, if necessary to achieve control objectives, are identified in description of its system.
Responding to Assessed RoMM
a. Determine whether SAAE regarding FS assertions available from user entity records &, if not,
b. Perform FAP to obtain SAAE or use another auditor to perform those procedures at SO.
Tests of Controls
When user auditor's risk assessment includes expectation that controls at SO are operating effecti
obtain audit evidence about operating effectiveness of those controls from following procedures:.
a. Obtaining a Type 2 report, if available
b. Performing appropriate TOCs at SO or
c. Using another auditor to perform TOCs at SO on behalf of user auditor.
Using a Type 2 report as audit evidence that controls at SO are operating effectively
If, user auditor plans to use Type 2 report as evidence that controls at SO are operating effecti
determine whether service auditor's report provides SAAE about effectiveness of controls by:
a. Evaluating description, design & operating effectiveness of controls at SO at date or period appropr
b. Determine whether complementary user entity controls identified by SQ relevant to user entity &,
if so, obtaining understanding of whether user entity designed & implemented such controls &,
if so, testing their operating effectiveness
c. Evaluating adequacy of time period covered by TOCs & time elapsed since performance of TOCS
d. Evaluating whether TOCs performed by service auditor & results, relevant to assertions in user ent
FS & provide SAAE.'
Fraud, NOCLAR & Uncorrected Misstatements in relation to Activities at SO
Inquire mgt of user entity whether SO has reported to user entity or user entity aware of, any fro
NOCLAR or uncorrected misstatements affecting FS of user entity.
✓ Evaluate how such matters affect NTE of FAP, including effect on conclusions & report.
Reporting by User Auditor
User auditor shall modify opinion as per SA 705 if unable to obtain SAAE regarding services prov
by 50 relevant to audit of user entity's FS.
User auditor shall not refer to work of service auditor in user auditor's report containing unmodi
opinion unless required by $L/R$. If such reference required by L/R, indicate reference doesn't dimi
user auditor's responsibility for audit opinion.
If reference relevant to understand modification of opinion, indicate in audit report, such refera
doesn't diminish user auditor's responsibility for opinion.

Responding to Assessed RoMM
a. Determine whether SAAE regarding FS assertions available from user entity records &, if not,

Tests of Controls
When user auditor's risk assessment includes expectation that controls at SO are operating effecti
obtain audit evidence about operating effectiveness of those controls from following procedures: -
a. Obtaining a Type 2 report, if available

If, user auditor plans to use Type 2 report as evidence that controls at SO are operating effectiv
determine whether service auditor's report provides SAAE about effectiveness of controls by:

FS & provide SAAE.
Fraud, NOCLAR & Uncorrected Misstatements in relation to Activities at SO
Inquire mgt of user entity whether SO has reported to user entity or user entity aware of, any fr
NOCLAR or uncorrected misstatements affecting FS of user entity.
Evaluate how such matters affect NTE of FAP, including effect on conclusions & report.
Reporting by User Auditor
User auditor shall modify opinion as per SA 705 if unable to obtain SAAE regarding services prov
by SO relevant to audit of user entity's FS.
User auditor shall not refer to work of service auditor in user auditor's report containing unmod
opinion unless required by L/R. If such reference required by $L/R$, indicate reference doesn't dim
user auditor's responsibility for audit opinion.
If reference relevant to understand modification of opinion, indicate in audit report, such refer
doesn't diminish user auditor's responsibility for opinion.
01 Standards on Auditing
SA 450: Evaluation of Misstatements Identified during the Audit
Objective
Learn
wah
Fun
valuate effect of identified misstatements on audit & uncorrected misstatements, if any, on FS.
Accumulation of Identified Misstatements
ccumulate misstatements identified during audit, other than clearly trivial.
lay designate an amount below which misstatements would be clearly trivial & wouldn't be accumulated
ecause auditor expects accumulation of such amounts clearly wouldn't have a material effect on FS
Clearly trivial" is not same as "not material" [ wholly different (smaller) order of magnitude + clearly
or aggregate & judged by any criteria of size, nature or circumstances]
consequential, individually
When any uncertainty about whether items are clearly trivial, matter considered not clearly trivial.
Difference between factual, judgmental & projected misstatements
Factual misstatements: Misstatements about which there is no doubt.
Tudgmental misstatements: Differences of judgments of mgt concerning $a/c$ estimates that auditor
Finds unreasonable, or selection/application of $a/c$ policies that auditor finds inappropriate.
Projected misstatements: Auditor's best estimate of misstatements in populations, involving projection
of misstatements in audit samples to entire populations from which samples were drawn.
Consideration of Identified Misstatements as Audit Progresses
Auditor shall determine whether overall audit strategy & audit plan need to be revised if:
Nature & circumstances of occurrence of identified misstatements indicate that other misstatements
may exist that, when aggregated with accumulated ones, could be material
OR
Aggregate of misstatements accumulated approaches materiality as per SA 320.
If, at auditor's request, mgt has examined & corrected detected misstatements, perform additional
audit procedures to determine whether misstatements remain.
Communication and Correction of Misstatements
Communicate all misstatements accumulated during audit with mgt.
Requesting mgt to correct those misstatements.
If mgt refuses, obtain understanding of reasons & evaluate whether FS are free from material
misstatement. [Maybe an indicator of mgt bias]
Uncorrected misstatements
Misstatements auditor has accumulated during audit & not corrected.
