Different forms of Network can be as under:-
Constituted as a mutual entity. Network itself will not carry out any professional practice.
Network can be constituted as partnership firm with total no. of partners does not exceed
Network can be constituted as LLP subject to CA Act & Rules & other applicable laws.
Network can be constituted as Co. subject to guidelines prescribed by ICAI for corporate f
practice & formation of mgt consultancy services Co. (Chap xvii)
Network Firms shall consist of sole Practitioner/proprietor, partnership or any such ent
professional accountants as may be permitted by the Act.
Firm is allowed to join only one network. Firms having common partners shall join only one Ne
Naming of Network
1. Network may have distinct name approved by ICAI. To distinguish a "Network" from a "CA firm",
"& Affiliates" used after name of network & words "& Co." /"& Associates" shall not be used.
2. Prescribed format of application for approval of Name for Network is Form 'A'.
3. ICAI shall approve/reject name of Network & intimate to Network at its address mentioned in
'A' within 30 days from date of receipt of said Form.
1. Mere approval of name of Network shall not entitle Network to carry on practice in its own nam
Registration of Network with entities in India
After name is approved, ICAI shall reserve name for period of 3 months from date of appro
Network shall get registered with ICAI by applying in Form B within 3 months, failing which
shall stand cancelled on expiry of said period.
Registration of Network is mandatory.
If different Indian firms are networked with a common Multinational Accounting Firm, the
be considered as part of network.
Listing of Network with entities outside India
Authorized representative of Indian Member firm (s)/Member constituting Network with e
outside India shall file declaration with ICAI in Form D for Listing such Network within 3
from date of entering into Network arrangement.
Proprietary/individual members, partnership firms & members in LLP or any such other entit
be permitted to join such network with entities outside India provided that they can join o
network & firms having common partners shall join only one such network.

Different forms of Network can be as under:-
Constituted as a mutual entity. Network itself will not carry out any professional practice.
• Network can be constituted as partnership firm with total no. of partners does not exceed 20.
Network can be constituted as LLP subject to CA Act & Rules & other applicable laws.
Network can be constituted as Co. subject to guidelines prescribed by ICAI for corporate form of
practice & formation of mgt consultancy services Co. (Chap xvii)
Network Firms shall consist of sole Practitioner/proprietor, partnership or any such entity of
professional accountants as may be permitted by the Act.
Firm is allowed to join only one network. Firms having common partners shall join only one Network
Naming of Network
1. Network may have distinct name approved by ICAI. To distinguish a "Network" from a "CA firm", word=
"& Affiliates" used after name of network & words "& Co." /"& Associates" shall not be used.
2. Prescribed format of application for approval of Name for Network is Form 'A'.
3. ICAI shall approve/reject name of Network & intimate to Network at its address mentioned in Form
'A' within 30 days from date of receipt of said Form.
4. Mere approval of name of Network shall not entitle Network to carry on practice in its own name.
Registration of Network with entities in India
1. After name is approved, ICAI shall reserve name for period of 3 months from date of approval.
2. Network shall get registered with ICAI by applying in Form B within 3 months, failing which name
shall stand cancelled on expiry of said period.
3. Registration of Network is mandatory.
4. If different Indian firms are networked with a common Multinational Accounting Firm, they shal
be considered as part of network.
Listing of Network with entities outside India
1. Authorized representative of Indian Member firm (s)/Member constituting Network with entitie
outside India shall file declaration with ICAI in Form D for Listing such Network within 30 day
from date of entering into Network arrangement.
2. Proprietary/individual members, partnership firms & members in LLP or any such other entity, sha
be permitted to join such network with entities outside India provided that they can join only on
network & firms having common partners shall join only one such network.

 Ethical Compliance: Necessary for network to comply with applicable ethical requirements of ICAI:
! If one firm of network is statutory auditor of entity then associate [including networked firms] or said
firm directly/indirectly not accept internal audit or book-keeping or other prohibited assignments. See-144
 Guidelines of ceiling on Non-audit fees is applicable in relation to Network as follows:
i. For a Network firm who is doing statutory audit (including its associate &/or firm(s) having common
partnership), it shall be same as mentioned in said notification & vito Audit fees
ii. For other firms of same Network collectively, it shall be 3 times of fee payable for carrying out
statutory audit of same undertaking/ Co.
3. In cases where rotation of firms prescribed, no member firm of network can accept appointment as
auditor in place of any member firm of network which is retiring.
4. Network may advertise to extent permitted by Advertisement Guidelines issued by ICAI. Firms
constituting network are permitted to use words "Network Firms" on their professional stationery.
5. Member firms of Network & Network shall comply with all Ethical Standards prescribed by Council.
If Change in constitution of registered Network due to entry or exit from Network, communicate to
ICAI by filing Form 'C' within 30 days from date of change in constitution.
Chapter XVI Logo Guidelines
Letters 'CA' in blue colour with a tri colour tick mark (upside down) with white background.
- Blue colour not only stands out on any background but also denotes creativity, innovativeness,
knowledge, integrity, trust, truth, stability, & depth.
- Upside-down tick mark, typically used by CAs, symbolise wisdom & value of professional.
- 'India' is also added, as it epitomizes ICAI's connection to India First approach & commitment to
serve Indian economy.
No change in font, spacing, dimensions, design & colours.
- Refrain from rotating or tilting logo clockwise & anti-clockwise.
- Logo should not be shrunk or distorted

INDIA
Note: Transition time of 1 year provided to use existing stationary/signage replacement etc.

articled/audit assistants, typists, peons, & others, not engaged in professional work

Other misconduct in relation to members of the Institute generally

ofother misconduct, if he:

is held guilty by any civil or criminal court for an offence which is punishable with

in opinion of Council, brings disrepute to profession or ICAI as a result of his

Audit Planning
Learr
Fun
Benefits of Planning
Nature & Extent of Planning vary as per:
Size & complexity of entity: More planning for large entity than small less complex entity
Past experience & expertise of engg. team also contributes to planning activities
 Change in circumstances
Reasons for
Changes to planning decisions
i) Result of
unexpected events
ii) Changes in
Conditions
Revise NTE of FAP based on revised Risk assessment.
iii) Audit evidence obtained from
audit procedures
This happens when info. coming to auditor differs from info. when he planned the audit procedures.
Eg. Audit evidence of substantive procedures contradict evidence from TOCs.
Separate
Planning - A Continuous Process
Planning is not discrete phase of audit but continual & iterative process.
Begins after completion of previous audit & continues until completion of current audit.
Includes considering timing of certain activities & audit procedures. Also involves Audit Programmi
Reed
Overall Audit Strategy & Audit Plan → Auditor's Responsibility
Auditor may discuss elements of planning with mgt to facilitate conduct of audit.
For eg - coordinate some planned audit procedures with work of entity's personnel.
When discussing overall audit strategy or plan, not compromise audit effectiveness.
Eg. discussing nature & timing of audit procedures with mgt may compromise audit effectiven
making it too predictable.
EP & other key engg team members involved in planning to enhance effectiveness & efficiency of p
Contents of Audit Plan
NTE of Risk Assessment Procedures (RAP) under SA 315
NTE of Further Audit Procedures (FAP) under SA 330
Other planned audit procedures to comply with SAS

04a Audit
With
Fun
Audit plan is more detailed than strategy that includes NTE of audit procedures
Planning for procedures takes place over the course of audit.
✓
Planning of RAP occurs early in audit.
Planning NTE of specific F.A.P depends on outcome of those R.A.P.
Auditor may begin execution of F.A.P for some ABCD before planning remaining F.A.P
Relationship between Overall Audit Strategy and Audit Plan
Audit strategy prepared before plan.
☑
It provides guidelines for developing audit plan.
Establishes scope & conduct of audit procedures & basis for developing detailed audit plan.
Audit plan is more detailed than overall audit strategy, includes NTE of audit procedures to obtain
Audit strategy & plan are inter-related because change in one would result in change in other.
reducef
CA. Sam developed audit strategy. While audit plan is developed, she decided materiality need
lowered as weaknesses in ICs highlighted in internal audit report. Deviation from audit strate
necessary. Therefore, first modify overall strategy & thereafter, prepare audit plan.
This shows audit strategy & plan are closely inter-related, change in one results in change in the
Summary: CR (High) → Lower materiality → Increase testing → Modify strategy → Plan
Factors while Establishing Overall Audit Strategy
a. Identify characteristics of engg. that define its scope s
b. Ascertain reporting objectives of engg. to plan timing of audit & communications required
c. Consider factors significant in directing engg. team's efforts ▷
d. Consider results of preliminary engagement activities & knowledge gained on other engagements
e. Ascertain NTE of resources necessary to perform engg.
Nature
Benefits of overall audit strategy
- Employment of qualitative resources: Experienced personnel for high risk areas & experts on com
matters like valuation/hedging.
dead line
Allocation of quantity of resources: Amount of resources allocated for each area.
Timing of deployment of resources: When resources to be deployed (interim stage or key cut-off
Mgt of resources: how resources are managed, directed & supervised, EP & manager reviews.
Considerations in Establishing Overall Audit Strategy: Examples of matters
Characteristics of engg. Scope
 FRF
mehnat
Industry specific reporting requirements
Expected audit coverage (no. & locations of components)
Extent to which components audited by other auditors
Nature of control relationships $b/w$ parent & components

necessary. Therefore, first modify overall strategy & thereafter, prepare audit plan.
Summary: CR (High) → Lower materiality → Increase testing → Modify strategy → Plan
Factors while Establishing Overall Audit Strategy
a. Identify characteristics of engg. that define its scope s
b. Ascertain reporting objectives of engg. to plan timing of audit & communications required
c. Consider factors significant in directing engg. team's efforts ▷
d. Consider results of preliminary engagement activities & knowledge gained on other engagements
e. Ascertain NTE of resources necessary to perform engg.
Nature

i. Employment of qualitative resources: Experienced personnel for high risk areas & experts on comp
matters like valuation/hedging.
deadlines

2. Control Risk (Do internal controls in place mitigate inherent risk)
Risk that IC system will not prevent, or detect & correct material misstatement on a timely basis.
Entity level controls like board oversight, IT general controls & HR policies are pervasive to all asser
whereas activity level controls relate to specific assertions.

L

Risk based Audit
Risk Based Audit (RBA) is approach that analyzes audit risks, set materiality & develops
programmes that allocate larger portion of resources to high risk areas.
Assessing ROMM in the FS
Steps of Risk Based Audit
Designing & performing FAP that respond to assessed risk & reduce it to acceptably low level
Issuing Audit report based on audit findings.
Explanation:
Risk Assessment:
☑ Performing client acceptance/continuance procedures
Planning overall engg.
Performing RAP to understand the business & identify inherent & control risks
Identifying relevant ICs & assessing their design & implementation
Assessing RoMM in FS.
Identifying significant risks that require special consideration
Communicating material weakness in Design & Implementation of IC to TCWG & Mgt
Assess RoMM at FS & assertion level.
Risk response:
Matters auditor should consider when planning audit procedures include:
Assertions that can't be addressed by substantive procedures alone. [Substantive not enough]
Eg. highly automated transactions with little or no manual intervention.
IC that, if tested, could reduce need/scope for other substantive procedures. [Reduce substa
Substantive analytical procedures that would reduce need/scope for other procedures. [Analy
Incorporate element of unpredictability in procedures performed. [Surprise checks]
Perform FAPs to address mgt override of controls or other fraud scenarios. [FAPS]
Perform specific procedures to address "significant risks" identified.
Reporting: Assess audit evidence & determine whether it's sufficient & appropriate to reduce
to acceptably low level.

Internal Control
Process designed, implemented & maintained by TCWG, mgt & others to provide reasonable assurance
about achievement of entity's objectives w.r.t
☑ safeguarding of assets,
✓

compliance with applicable laws and regulations,

When obtaining evidence about effective operation of ICs, consider: how they were applied, consistency
with which they were applied during the period & by whom they were applied.
Objectives of internal controls relating to accounting system are:
i. Transactions are executed through general/specific mgt authorization.
i. All transactions promptly recorded for preparation of financial info. & maintain accountability of assets.
 . Assets & records safeguarded from unauthorized disposition, use or access.
. Assets are verified at reasonable intervals & appropriate action taken w.r.t discrepancies.
Basic Accounting Control Objectives to be achieved by any $a/c$ control system are:
Whether all transactions are: recorded, real, properly valued, timely recorded, properly posted,
properly classified and disclosed & summarized
Limitations of Internal Control
Mgt consideration IC cost doesn't exceed benefit derived from it
Most IC not directed at unusual transactions. Also, there's potential of human error.
Possibility of circumvention of IC through collusion with employees or $3^{rd}$ parties
Person responsible for exercising IC could abuse responsibility e.g. mgt override of IC
Manipulation by mgt w.r.t transactions/estimates & judgements in preparation of FS.
Learn with Fun
Structure of Internal Control [Coverage of Policies & Procedures]
Segregation of Duties:
Following functions are segregated: Authorisation → Execution of transactions  Physical custody of
related assets → Maintenance of records & documents. Periodic rotation of duties is also desirable.
Authorisation of transactions: Authorizations issued by persons acting within scope of authority &
transactions confirm to terms of authorizations.

4. Accountability & safeguarding of assets
Assets like cash, inventories, investment scrips require frequent physical verification with books
Assets considered sensitive & susceptible to error need to be reconciled more frequently
Only authorised personnel should be given access to asset
✓ Effective controls over physical custody of cash, inventories, investments & other fixed assets.

Components of IC [Checking adequacy of IC]
Control Environment >> Risk Assess Process >> Control Activities >> Info. System >> Monitori
Control Environment Elements:
Communication & enforcement of integrity & ethical values: Effectiveness of ICs depends on inte
ethical values of people. Eg. Policy statements, codes of conduct etc.
Commitment to competence: Knowledge & skills to accomplish the job tasks.
Participation by TCWG: Oversight of whistle blower procedures & reviewing effectiveness of IC
 Mgt philosophy & operating style: Mgt's attitudes & actions toward FR.
Org. structure: Consider key areas of authority & responsibility & lines of reporting.
Assignment of authority & responsibility: Policies relating to appt. business practices, knowl
experience of key personnel, & resources carrying out duties.
Integrity & ethics + Competence → TCWG + Mgt → Org Structure + Authority & Responsibi
Key components to assess & evaluate controls environment
Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs): Well-defined SOPs helps outline role, responsibilities,
& controls. SOPs promote consistent application of controls even during employee turnover.
Delegation of financial powers document: Document delegation of powers to allow controls to op
Enterprise Risk Mgt: Org should have robust process to identify & mitigate risks
Segregation of Job Responsibilities: No 2 commercial activities should be done by same person
Job rotation in sensitive areas: A job carried by person for long may lead to complacency & mis
IT based controls: Embed controls within system instead of being dependent on humans.
Eg. for customer invoicing, application of correct rates or credit control can be done through IT

1. Control Environment Elements:
α.
Communication & enforcement of integrity & ethical values: Effectiveness of ICs depends on integrity &
ethical values of people. Eg. Policy statements, codes of conduct etc.
b. Commitment to competence: Knowledge & skills to accomplish the job tasks.
c. Participation by TCWG: Oversight of whistle blower procedures & reviewing effectiveness of IC.

Integrity & ethics + Competence → TCWG + Mgt  Org Structure + Authority & Responsibility
Key components to assess & evaluate controls environment
1. Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs): Well-defined SOPs helps outline role, responsibilities, process
& controls. SOPs promote consistent application of controls even during employee turnover.
2. Delegation of financial powers document: Document delegation of powers to allow controls to operate.
3. Enterprise Risk Mgt: Org should have robust process to identify & mitigate risks
4. Segregation of Job Responsibilities: No 2 commercial activities should be done by same person.
5. Job rotation in sensitive areas: A job carried by person for long may lead to complacency & misuse.
6. IT based controls: Embed controls within system instead of being dependent on humans.
Eg. for customer invoicing, application of correct rates or credit control can be done through IT systen
2. Entity's Risk Assessment Procedures
Identify business risks relevant to FS preparation → Assess likelihood & significance → Actions to
Respond & manage risks
4b.5
Learn
Wah
Fun
04b Risk Assessment & Internal Control
Risk can arise or change due to certain circumstances
New technology: New technologies in production processes or info, systems may change IC risks,
New or revamped info. Systems: Significant & rapid changes in info, systems can change IC risks.
New personnel: New personnel may have a different focus or understanding of IC.
Rapid growth: Significant & rapid expansion of operations can strain ICs & increase risk of IC breakdown.
Changes in operating environment: Can result in changes in competitive pressures & different risks.
Corporate restructurings: Restructurings may include staff reductions & changes in supervision &
segregation of duties that may change the IC risks.
New $a/c$ pronouncements: Adoption of new or changing $a/c$ principles may affect FS risks.
SCAM 1992
THE BARGUAR METASTNY
Control Activities [Policies & procedures covering following areas]
erformance Reviews: Review & analysis of Actual performance vs budgets, forecasts & prior period
erformance. Investigate & take corrective actions.
fo. Processing: Two categories of info. system control activities are:
eneral IT Controls (relate to many applications & support functioning of application controls)
controls over program change, restrict access to programs or data, implementation of new software
plications & controls over system.
plication controls (apply to individual applications)
Checking arithmetical accuracy of records, automated controls like edit checks of input data
merical sequence checks & manual follow-up of exception reports.
sical Controls:
Physical security of assets,

04b Risk Assessment & Internal Control
Risk can arise or change due to certain circumstances
a. New technology: New technologies in production processes or info. systems may change IC risks.
b. New or revamped info. Systems: Significant & rapid changes in info. systems can change IC risks,
c. New personnel: New personnel may have a different focus or understanding of IC.
d. Rapid growth: Significant & rapid expansion of operations can strain ICs & increase risk of IC breakdown,
e. Changes in operating environment: Can result in changes in competitive pressures & different risks.
f. Corporate restructurings: Restructurings may include staff reductions & changes in supervision &
segregation of duties that may change the IC risks.
g. New $a/c$ pronouncements: Adoption of new or changing $a/c$ principles may affect FS risks.
Control Activitie's [Policies & procedures covering following areas]
SCAM 1992
 
a. Performance Reviews: Review & analysis of Actual performance vs budgets, forecasts & prior period
performance. Investigate & take corrective actions.
 . Info. Processing: Two categories of info, system control activities are:
General IT Controls (relate to many applications & support functioning of application controls)
Eg. controls over program change, restrict access to programs or data, implementation of new software
applications & controls over system.
Application controls (apply to individual applications)
Eg. Checking arithmetical accuracy of records, automated controls like edit checks of input data &
numerical sequence checks & manual follow-up of exception reports.
Physical Controls:
✓ Physical security of assets,
✓ Authorisation for access to computer prog. & data files
✓ Periodic counting & comparison with amounts shown on control records.
For eg. Comparing results of cash, inventory & security counts with $a/c$ records.
Segregation of duties: Assign different people for authorising, recording & custody of assets.
Info. System, Including Related Business Processes, Relevant to Financial Reporting & Communication
Info. system relevant to FR objectives, which includes FR system, includes methods & records that:
Identify & record all valid transactions.
Describe transactions in sufficient detail to permit proper classification of transactions for FR.
Measure value of transactions for recording their proper monetary value in FS.
Determine time period in which transactions occurred for recording transactions in proper
$a/c$
period.
wonctions & related disclosures in FS.
04b Risk Assessment & Internal Control
Monitoring of Controls
Lea
Fu
Considering whether ICs are operating as intended & they are modified for changes in conditions.
Objectives of internal check system:
Internal Check System
Prevent & avoid misappropriation or embezzlement of cash & falsification of accounts.
Avoid & minimize possibility of errors & fraud by any staff.
Detect error & frauds with ease.
Locate responsibility area or stages where actual fraud & error occurs.
Increase efficiency of staff within the organization.
Protect integrity of business by ensuring $a/cs$ are always subject to proper scrutiny & check.
Effectiveness of Internal Check depends on following considerations:
Clarity of responsibility: Responsibility of different people properly defined.
Division of work: Segregation of work to ensure free flow & job rotation.
Standardisation: Process of accounting should be standardised.
Appraisal: Periodic review of chain of operations & work flow by preparing audit flow chart.
Eg. To review internal controls of Sales Deptt., you visited & noticed work division as follows:
(1) An officer was handling the sales ledger and cash receipts.
(2) Another official was handling dispatch of goods and issuance of Delivery challans.
(3) One more officer was there to handle customer/ debtor accounts and issue of receipts.
Discuss general condition pertaining to internal check in IC system.
Do you think there was proper division of work? If not, why?
General condition pertaining to internal check system summarized as under:
No complete control: No single person should have complete control over any important as
business operation. Every employee's action should come under review of another person.
Job rotation: Staff duties should be rotated time to time so that members don't perform same
for a considerable length of time.
Leave once in a year: Every staff member should be encouraged to go on leave at least once a y
No access to books: Person with physical custody must have no access to books.
$A/c$ control for each class of asset with periodical inspection to establish their physical condi-
Budgetary controls should be exercised & wide deviations should be reconciled.
Mechanical devices to prevent misappropriation of cash
Inventory taking at close of year, trading activities be suspended & done by staff of several s
organization.
In given scenario, Co. has not done proper division of work as:
receipts of cash should not be handled by official handling sales ledger and
delivery challans verified by authorised official other than officer handling despatch of good

Effectiveness of Internal Check depends on following considerations:
i. Clarity of responsibility: Responsibility of different people properly defined.

iii. Standardisation: Process of accounting should be standardised.
iv. Appraisal: Periodic review of chain of operations & work flow by preparing audit flow chart.

General condition pertaining to internal check system summarized as under:
i. No complete control: No single person should have complete control over any important aspec
business operation. Every employee's action should come under review of another person.

for a considerable length of time.

04b Risk Assessment & Internal Control
Manual elements in IC more suitable where judgment required such as following circumstances:
Large, unusual or non-recurring transactions.
 Circumstances where errors are difficult to define, anticipate or predict.
In changing circumstances that require control response outside scope of existing automated control
In monitoring the effectiveness of automated controls.
Review of IC enables auditor
to formulate his opinion as to the reliance he may place on system itself i.e. whether system is such as
would enable mgt to produce a true & fair set of FS &
to locate the areas of weakness in the system so that audit programme & NTE of substantive &
compliance audit procedures can be adjusted.
Eg. 1. If not satisfied with IC of debtors, may have wider coverage for confirmation of debtors.
2. Normally, investments & cash physically verified at period end & this is known to the client.
If weakness in IC he may provide for surprise cash count or investment verification.
3. If due to weak ICs possibility of wrong billing to customers, may compare invoices with forwardin
notes, for checking of extensions & castings of invoices.
Basic Assumptions about a Good Internal Control Questionnaire
In standardized IC questionnaire, certain basic assumptions about elements of good control are -
Certain procedures used by most business concerns are essential in achieving reliable IC.
 Daily banking of receipts or balancing of cash book or periodic reconciliations
ivision of duties & responsibilities
o single person with thrust of completing a transaction all by himself
mployees with accounting function not assigned custodian function
ork performed by one reviewed by another
oper documentation & recording of transactions
tes:
-1st year engagements: issue of questionnaire is necessary.
- subsequent years: May request client to confirm whether any change in business that necessita-
nge in ICs or otherwise IC has undergone a change.

In changing circumstances that require control response outside scope of existing automated control

Review of IC enables auditor -
i. to formulate his opinion as to the reliance he may place on system itself i.e. whether system is such
would enable mgt to produce a true & fair set of FS &
ii. to locate the areas of weakness in the system so that audit programme & NTE of substantive
compliance audit procedures can be adjusted.
Eg. 1. If not satisfied with IC of debtors, may have wider coverage for confirmation of debtors.
2. Normally, investments & cash physically verified at period end & this is known to the client.
If weakness in IC he may provide for surprise cash count or investment verification.
3. If due to weak ICs possibility of wrong billing to customers, may compare invoices with forwardi
notes, for checking of extensions & castings of invoices.
Basic Assumptions about a Good Internal Control Questionnaire
In standardized IC questionnaire, certain basic assumptions about elements of good control are -
Certain procedures used by most business concerns are essential in achieving reliable IC.
Eg Daily banking of receipts or balancing of cash book or periodic reconciliations
Division of duties & responsibilities
No single person with thrust of completing a transaction all by himself
Employees with accounting function not assigned custodian function
Work performed by one reviewed by another
Proper documentation & recording of transactions
Notes:
For 1st year engagements: issue of questionnaire is necessary.
For subsequent years: May request client to confirm whether any change in business that necessitate
change in ICs or otherwise IC has undergone a change.

Material Weakness
Defined as absence of adequate ICs that increases possibility of errors & frauds in FS.
Eg. if age-wise analysis of debtors not performed → may result in inadequate provision of bad de
Important points w.r.t such letter are as follows:
Letter lists down areas of weaknesses & offers suggestions for improvement.
Clearly indicate that it discusses only weaknesses which have come to attention of auditor & exam
not designed to determine adequacy of IC for mgt.
This letter serves as valuable reference document for revising system & insisting on its
implementation.
Also serve to minimize legal liability due to major defalcation/other loss resulting from a weaknes
Areas of weakness + Suggestions >> Attention >> Valuable reference doc >> Minimise legal liab
Case Study on Internal Control: Entertainment Centres
Y Co. Ltd. has 5 entertainment centres. Collections made in cash. Specify IC system for money col
Printing of tickets: Serially numbered pre-printed tickets should be used & designed in a way t
ticket cannot be duplicated by others to avoid forgery.
Ticket sales: Sale of tickets from Central ticket office, preferably through machines. Proper
over the keys of the machines.
Daily cash reconciliation: Cash collected should be reconciled with no. of tickets sold.
Daily banking: Each day's collection should be deposited in bank on next working day of bank. Cas
be in custody of properly authorized person preferably in joint custody.
Entrance tickets should be cancelled at entrance gate when public enters centre.
Advance booking: If advance booking facility available, ensure all advance booked tickets are par

Basic distinction between IC questionnaire & check list are as under:
1. ICQ includes large no of detailed questions, check list contains ques. related to main control objective.
2. In ICQ, ques, answered by Co. executives. In check list, they are answered by auditor/auditor staff.
3. 'No' in ICQ does indicate a weakness but significance of that weakness is not revealed. In check list,
specific statement is required for material weakness.
Flow chart depiction of flow of documents:
i. at what point a document is raised internally or received from external sources
ii. no. of copies in which a document is raised or received
iii. intermediate stages through which document & the activity pass
iv. distribution of documents to various sections, department or operations
v. checking authorisation & matching at relevant stages
vi. filing of the documents &
vii. final disposal by sending out or destruction
Material Weakness
Defined as absence of adequate ICs that increases possibility of errors & frauds in FS.
Eg. if age-wise analysis of debtors not performed → may result in inadequate provision of bad debts.
Important points w.r.t such letter are as follows:
a. Letter lists down areas of weaknesses & offers suggestions for improvement.
b. Clearly indicate that it discusses only weaknesses which have come to attention of auditor & examination
not designed to determine adequacy of IC for mgt.
c. This letter serves as valuable reference document for revising system & insisting on its strict
implementation.
d. Also serve to minimize legal liability due to major defalcation/other loss resulting from a weakness in IC
Areas of weakness + Suggestions >> Attention >> Valuable reference doc >> Minimise legal liability
Case Study on Internal Control: Entertainment Centres
Y Co. Ltd. has 5 entertainment centres. Collections made in cash. Specify IC system for money collection
a. Printing of tickets: Serially numbered pre-printed tickets should be used & designed in a way that any
ticket cannot be duplicated by others to avoid forgery.
b. Ticket sales: Sale of tickets from Central ticket office, preferably through machines. Proper contro
over the keys of the machines.
c. Daily cash reconciliation: Cash collected should be reconciled with no. of tickets sold.
d. Daily banking: Each day's collection should be deposited in bank on next working day of bank, Cash shoul
be in custody of properly authorized person preferably in joint custody.
e. Entrance tickets should be cancelled at entrance gate when public enters centre.
f. Advance booking: If advance booking facility available, ensure all advance booked tickets are paid for.

Discounts & free pass: Discount policy for group booking should be properly authorized & sig

Surprise checks: Internal audit system should carry out periodic surprise checks for cash counts, de
banking, reconciliation & stock of unsold tickets etc.
International Internal Control Frameworks
COSO Framework [IC Framework by Committee of Sponsoring Organisations of Treadway Commission
Components of IC: Control Environment, Risk Assessment, Control Activities, Information
Communication, Monitoring
3 categories of objectives:
Operating objectives: effectiveness & efficiency of operations
eporting objectives: internal & external financial & non-financial reporting to stakeholders
ompliance objectives: compliance with laws & regulations
Co Framework (Issued by Canadian Institute of Chartered Accountants)
outlines criteria for effective control in the following 4 areas:
urpose 2. Commitment 3. Capability 4.Monitoring and Learning
BIT Framework (Control Objectives for Information and Related Technology)
IT has 34 high-level processes that cover 210 control objectives
globally by all managers responsible for IT business processes.
es quality, control & reliability (QCR) of info. systems, most important for every modern business,
es organization on how to use IT resources.
ssist businesses in complying with laws, regulations, & contractual arrangements.
mes Oxley Section 404
rform assessment of IC over financial reporting (ICFR) including tests of controls.
clude in its annual report assessment of ICFR.
al auditors provide two opinions as part of a single integrated audit of Co:
Independent opinion on effectiveness of system of ICFR.
Traditional opinion on FS.
"It's going to be hard, but Not impossible"

International Internal Control Frameworks
COSO Framework [IC Framework by Committee of Sponsoring Organisations of Treadway Commission]
Components of IC: Control Environment, Risk Assessment, Control Activities, Information &
Communication, Monitoring
3 categories of objectives:
a. Operating objectives: effectiveness & efficiency of operations
b. Reporting objectives: internal & external financial & non-financial reporting to stakeholders
c. Compliance objectives: compliance with laws & regulations
CoCo Framework (Issued by Canadian Institute of Chartered Accountants)
It outlines criteria for effective control in the following 4 areas:
1.Purpose 2. Commitment 3. Capability 4.Monitoring and Learning
COBIT Framework (Control Objectives for Information and Related Technology)
COBIT has 34 high-level processes that cover 210 control objectives
Used globally by all managers responsible for IT business processes.
Ensures quality, control & reliability (QCR) of info. systems, most important for every modern business.
Guides organization on how to use IT resources.
Can assist businesses in complying with laws, regulations, & contractual arrangements.

Agt perform assessment of IC over financial reporting (ICFR) including tests of controls.

CHAPTER
05
Group Audit
Mandatory under Companies Act,2013
Where Co. has one/more subsidiaries, including JVs & associates, prepare CFS of Co. & all its subsi
Provisions of preparation, adoption & audit of FS of Holding Co. shall apply mutatis mutandis to C
CFS shall also be approved by BODs with SFS & laid before AGM of Co. with SFS.
Also attach separate statement containing salient features of FS of subsidiary(ies) in Form AOC
CFS shall be made as per Schedule III & applicable AS.
Co. which isn't required to prepare CFS as per AS, comply with Schedule III
Requirement to prepare CFS doesn't apply if ALL following conditions met [May'25]
Wholly or partially owned subsy & all its other members been intimated in writing & for which Pr
Delivery is available with Co. & they don't object to Co. not presenting CFS.
NIT
Co. whose securities not listed or in process of listing in any stock exchange (in or outside India)
Ultimate or intermediary holding Co. files CFS with ROC in compliance with AS
Note: Investment entity need not present CFS if it measures all subsidiaries at Fair value throug
 
Investment Entity is an entity that:
a. obtains funds from investors for providing investment mgt services
b. business purpose is to invest funds for returns from capital appreciation, investment income, or b
c. measures & evaluates performance of substantially all of investments on fair value basis.
Note: Parent of Investment entity shall prepare CFS unless it's also an Investment entity.
Eg. Parent Ltd acquired 51% shares of Child Ltd during year ended 31.3.24. During FY 2024-25
shares of Child Ltd were sold by Parent Ltd. Parent Ltd while preparing FS for year ended 31.
31.3.25 didn't consider FS of Child Ltd for consolidation. As auditor how would you deal with it?
If As titis Exempt No such exerup
For Ind As
As per Ind AS 110, there's no exemption for 'temporary control' or "for operation under sever
term funds transfer restrictions" & consolidation is mandatory.
However, as per sec 129(3) of Cos. Act, 2013 where Co. having subsidiary, is not required to prepa
under AS. It shall comply with provisions of CFS provided in Schedule III.
Facts
In given case, Parent Ltd acquired 51% shares of Child Ltd during the year ended 31.03.24 and so
shares during the year ended 31.03.25. Parent Ltd did not consolidate F.S. of Child Ltd for year
31.03.24 & 31.03.25. Intention of Parent Ltd is quite clear that control in Child Ltd is temporar
disposed off acquired shares in next year of its purchase.
Conclusion
Parent Ltd is required to prepare CFS as per Ind AS 110 as exemption for 'temporary control',
operation under severe long-term funds transfer restrictions" is not available under Ind AS 110.
It states that "Consolidation of an investee shall begin from date investor obtains control of in
and cease when investor loses control of investee".

Investment Entity is an entity that:
a. obtains funds from investors for providing investment mgt services
b. business purpose is to invest funds for returns from capital appreciation, investment income, or both &
c. measures & evaluates performance of substantially all of investments on fair value basis.
Note: Parent of Investment entity shall prepare CFS unless it's also an Investment entity.
Eg. Parent Ltd acquired 51% shares of Child Ltd during year ended 31.3.24. During FY 2024-25, 20%
shares of Child Ltd were sold by Parent Ltd. Parent Ltd while preparing FS for year ended 31.3.24 &
31.3.25 didn't consider FS of Child Ltd for consolidation. As auditor how would you deal with it?
If As titis Exempt
Fon Indias No such exemption.
As per Ind AS 110, there's no exemption for 'temporary control' or "for operation under severe long-
term funds transfer restrictions" & consolidation is mandatory.
However, as per sec 129(3) of Cos. Act, 2013 where Co. having subsidiary, is not required to prepare CFS
under AS. It shall comply with provisions of CFS provided in Schedule III.
Facts
T
In given case, Parent Ltd acquired 51% shares of Child Ltd during the year ended 31.03.24 and sold 20%
shares during the year ended 31.03.25. Parent Ltd did not consolidate F.S. of Child Ltd for year ende
31.03.24 & 31.03.25. Intention of Parent Ltd is quite clear that control in Child Ltd is temporary as i
disposed off acquired shares in next year of its purchase.
Conclusion
Parent Ltd is required to prepare CFS as per Ind AS 110 as exemption for 'temporary control , or "fo
operation under severe long-term funds transfer restrictions" is not available under Ind AS 110.
It states that "Consolidation of an investee shall begin from date investor obtains control of investe
and cease when investor loses control of investee".

Identify componenets >> Obtain info >> consol. Adj.  a/c policies + GAAP Adj. » Segment + Rp
Note: Parent issues instructions to mgt of component specifying financial info. of components to be
Read included in CFS. These cover $a/c$ policies, statutory & other disclosures, reportable segments, related
parties & RPTs & reporting timetable.
Auditor's objectives in audit of CFS
a. to satisfy that CFS have been prepared as per AFRF
b. to express opinion on true & fair view presented by CFS
c. to enquire into matters specified in Sec 143(1) of Companies Act, 2013 &
d. to report on matters given in Sec 143(3) of Companies Act, 2013
e. Also validate requirement of preparation of CFS for company as per AFRF.
Read
Audit Considerations
Following features of CFS have an impact on related audit procedures:
a. CFS prepared on the basis of SFS of parent & its components, using procedures of AS under AFRF &
b. May use work of other auditors as per SA unless auditor of CFS is also auditor of other components.
CFS are prepared using SFS of parent & its components & also other financial info.
'Other financial info.' includes disclosures in CFS about components, items included in CFS to which
different $a/c$ policies applied, adjustments for significant transactions or other events between FS of
parent & its components etc. This 'other financial info." will be additionally disclosed.
Assess based on his work alone he would be able to express opinion on true & fair view presented by CFS.
If not, consider using work of 'other auditors'.
Such 'other auditors' might be statutory auditors of SFS of components or auditors appointed
specifically for assisting auditor of CFS (principal auditor).
Auditor's considerations about Materiality
For audit of SFS, compute materiality for each component on a standalone basis.
For audit of CFS:
(2
• Compute materiality for group as a whole. This materiality used to assess appropriateness of
consolidation adjustments (i.e. permanent & current period consolidation adjustments) made by mgt.

Note: While considering observations,i.e. Modification/EOM/OM para of component auditor in his r
on SFS, materiality will be considered. This is as per principles of SA 600.

CFS AIR
Planning Audit of CFS
Understand group structure & group-wide controls including assessment of IT system & related g
& applications IT related controls (manual & automated) for consolidation process
Understand $a/c$ policies of parent & its components & consolidation process
Determining NTE of audit procedures to be performed based on risk assessment in consolidation pr
Determining extent of use of other auditor's work in audit &
Coordinating work to be performed.
How Auditor ensures completeness of Components included in CFS?
review working papers for prior years for known components
review parent's procedures for identification of components
make inquiries of mgt to identify any new components or any component which goes out of CFS
review investments of parent & its components to determine shareholding in other entities
review joint ventures & joint arrangements

Planning Audit of CFS
H
a. Understand group structure & group-wide controls including assessment of IT system & related gene
& applications IT related controls (manual & automated) for consolidation process
b. Understand $a/c$ policies of parent & its components & consolidation process
c. Determining NTE of audit procedures to be performed based on risk assessment in consolidation proce
fd. Determining extent of use of other auditor's work in audit &
e. Coordinating work to be performed.
How Auditor ensures completeness of Components included in CFS?
a. review working papers for prior years for known components
b. review parent's procedures for identification of components
c. make inquiries of mgt to identify any new components or any component which goes out of CFS
d. review investments of parent & its components to determine shareholding in other entities
e. review joint ventures & joint arrangements
f. review other arrangements entered by parent not included in CFS
g. Identify changes in shareholding that have taken place during reporting period

56
Adjustments for preparation of CFS are made in memorandum records
Auditor should review memorandum records to verify adjustment entries made in preparation
Apart from reviewing memorandum records, auditor should inter alia:
verify intra group transactions & account balances have been eliminated
verify that CFS prepared using uniform a/c policies for like transactions & other events
verify that adequate disclosures have been made as per AS 21 in CFS of application of differ
policies if impracticable to harmonize them.
Its impracticable when entity can't apply it after making every reasonable effort to do so but un
AS, ensure appropriate adjustments made to group member's FS to ensure conformity with gro
policies as per Ind AS 110
verify adjustments to harmonise different a/c policies including adjustments made by mgt to co
component's FS prepared under the component's GAAP to GAAP under which CFS are prepared
verify calculation of minorities/non-controlling interest
verify adjustments for deferred tax due to temporary differences of elimination of P&L resultin
intergroup transactions (where parent's accounts are maintained in Ind AS)
verify that income & expenses of subsidiary are included in CFS from date it gains control until t
when entity ceases to control the subsidiary & further such income & expenses are based on ama
assets & liabilities recognised in CFS at the acquisition date.
Note:
Difference between reporting dates of component FS & date of CFS should not be more than 6
in case of FS under AS & 3 months under Ind AS.
Goodwill is tested for impairment loss on every BS Date.
If impairment loss in goodwill of component determined in foreign currency, verify if amount o
ocal currency need to be adjusted from currency translation reserve due to movement in exchar
Following info. also required to be disclosed in CFS separately for parent & its components (in
Foreign component) which has been consolidated:
a. amt of net assets & net assets as percentage of consolidated net assets
D. amt of share in P&L & percentage of P&L as percentage of consolidated P&L
 . amount in OCI & percentage of OCI as a percentage of Consolidated OCI.

05 Group Audit
Examples of info. given in SFS of parent or subsidiaries, need not be given in CFS
$a/c$
i. Source of bonus shares eg. Capitalization of profits or reserves or securities premium
ii. Disclosure of unutilized monies out of issue indicating form in which they have been invested
iii. Disclosure under MSME Development Act 2006
iv. Value of imports on CIF basis by Co. during FY for: [CIF → Cost Insurance Freight]
a) Raw material
b) Components & spare parts
c) Capital Goods
v. Expenditure in forex during FY for Royalty, know how etc
vi. Value of imported & indigenous Raw material, spare parts & components consumed & percentage of each
to total consumption.
vii. Dividend remitted in foreign currencies during the year, mention no. of non-resident shareholders, no.
of shares held & year to which dividends related.
viii. Statement of investments, showing names of bodies corporate in whose shares or debentures,
investments have been made & nature & extent of investment made in each body corporate.
Management Representations regarding CFS+Rogent Responsibilines
a. Completeness of components included in CFS
b. Appropriateness & completeness of permanent & current period consolidation adjustments, including
elimination of intra-group transactions
Ind AS-
c. Identification of reportable segments for segmental reporting 108
d. Identification of related parties & related party transactions for reporting
Rnd Rg4
Expected
Reporting
When Parent's Auditor is also Auditor of all its Components
✓ Report whether procedures for preparation & presentation of CFS as per AS followed.
✓ For departure/deviation, consider SA 705 in $A/R$ so that users are aware of such deviation.
 Issue an $A/R$ expressing opinion whether CFS give true & fair view of state of affair of Group as on BS
date & whether consolidated P&L gives true & fair view of results of consolidated P&L of Group.
 Where CFS also include cash flow statement, also give opinion on true & fair view of cash flows.
When Parent's Auditor not the Auditor of all its Components
Consider the requirement of SA 600.
As per SA 706, if auditor makes reference to work of other auditor in his $A/R$ on CFS, disclose clearly
magnitude of portion of FS audited by other auditor.
This may be done by stating aggregate amounts or percentage of total assets, revenues & cash flows
included in CFS not audited by parent's auditor.
Total assets, revenues & cash flows should be presented before giving effect to permanent & current
period consolidation adjustments.

When Component(s) Auditor Reports under an Auditing Framework Different from Parent
Audits of FS, including CFS performed as per audit standards generally accepted in India (Indian GA
To maintain consistency of auditing framework & enable parent auditor to rely & refer to other audi

Indian GAAS.

CHAPTER
06a
Bank Audit
(5-8) Marks
Characteristics distinguishing banks from other commercial enterprises
Custody of many monetary items, like cash, whose physical security to be ensured.
Large volume & variety of transactions.
Transactions by customer without bank's employees intervention. Eg. Over Internet or mobile or
Geographically dispersed branches & departments.
If Special Audit Considerations
Special audit considerations arise in audit of banks because of:
Particular nature of risks associated with transactions
Scale of banking operations & significant exposures which can arise in short period
Extensive dependence on IT to process transactions
Continuing development of new products & services not matched by a/c principles & auditing prac
volution of technology & Net Banking & Mobiles has exposed banks to huge operational & financ
ffect of statutory & regulatory requirements
 
Form & Content of FS
: Form A & P&L: Form B of Third Schedule to Banking Regulation Act, 1949

Basis of computation of audit fee & travel, related allowances & other expense reimbursement
ter of acceptance, declaration of fidelity & secrecy, etc.
Loyalty
: For statutory branch auditors (SBAs), similar appointment letter given except particulars of
ors & division of work. SBA is done by a single firm of CAS.

Special considerations in IT Environment
Considering importance of IT systems, bank should share detailed info. with auditors like: -
Overall IT policy, structure & environment of Bank's IT system
Data processing & interface under various systems
Data integrity & security
Controls over key aspects, expense booking, overdue identification etc.
Controls on recording of e-banking & internet banking products
6a.2
06a Bank Audit
Note:
Overall review of IT & computerized $a/c$ system taken at HO level.
Branch auditors don't have access to IT policy & processes.
Based on guidance from SCAs, branch auditors review data & TOCs & substantive checking of
transactions at branch level & results shared with SCAS.
Key control aspects to address while undertaking audit in a Computerised bank.
 Ensure authorised, accurate & complete data available for processing
 Ensure in case of power failure, system restarts without affecting completion of entries & recor
Verify access controls of staff match with their responsibilities
Verify segregation of duties ensured while granting system access to users
Ensure system prevents unauthorised amendments to programme
Data → power failure → access controls + SOD → Prevent unauthorised amendments
Risk Based Internal Audit (RBIA)
Central audit & inspection deptt. in banks headed by Chief Audit Executive. Responsible for doing
Risk assessment process includes:
Identification of inherent business risks in activities of branches (Business risk)
Assessment of control systems effectiveness for monitoring inherent risks (Control risk)
Making assessment of level & direction of risk areas & overall business risk & control risk
Draw risk matrix considering factors viz. Risk of branch
Internal Controls
General:
Staff of bank shifted from one position to another without prior notice.
Jork of one person checked by another person (Internal check)
rithmetical accuracy of books proved independently every day.
I bank forms (e.g. Cheque books, demand draft etc) should be in possession of officer & another
ould verify issuance & stock of such stationery.
il opened by responsible officer. Signatures on letters & advices received from other bank c
officer with signature book.
nature & telegraphic code book kept with responsible officers & access only to authorised of
urance policies against loss due to fire, natural calamities, theft & employees' infidelity.
prise inspection of HO & Branches by Internal Audit Deptt.
dhokha
$Shift/Check/Accuracy\rightarrow Forins\rightarrow Mail\rightarrow Sign/Code~book\rightarrow Insurance~Policies\rightarrow Surpri\epsilon$

Borrowings from abroad by Indian banks considered as 'liabilities to other' & at gross level unl
"liabilities towards banking system in India', which can be netted off against 'assets towards bank
system in India'. Thus, adverse balances in Nostro Mirror $a/c$ to be considered as 'Liabilities to other
Reconciliation of Nostro a/c (with Nostro Mirror $a/cs$) to be scrutinized carefully for any inwar
emittances received on behalf of customers / constituents of bank &/or any other debit (inwa
ntries unaccounted & liabilities for the bank.

inimum Eligible Credit (EC) & outstanding Long-Term Bonds (LB) to finance Infrastructure loans
fordable housing loan.
gible amount of incremental FCNR(B) & NRE deposits of maturities of 3 years & above,
to verify loans out of FCNR(B) deposits & inter- bank Foreign Currency (IBFC) deposits for report
Form A return. Banks should convert forex assets/liabilities (including borrowings) in USD, GBP. J
uro into INR at RBI reference rate. For other currencies convert into USD using New York rate.

/USD & other major currencies",

sider at gross.
Recen
Net cr. balance in branch adjustment a/cs. Cr. entries in the a/c outstanding for >5 yrs consider at
Interest accrued on deposits calculated on each reporting fortnight, whether or not accounted, so that
bank's liability is reflected in total NDTL of the same fortnightly return,
Cash collaterals received under collateralized derivative transactions are in nature of 'outside liabilities'.
Borrowings from abroad by Indian banks considered as 'liabilities to other' & at gross level unlike
'liabilities towards banking system in India', which can be netted off against 'assets towards banking
system in India'. Thus, adverse balances in Nostro Mirror $a/c$ to be considered as 'Liabilities to other'.
Reconciliation of Nostro a/c (with Nostro Mirror $a/cs$) to be scrutinized carefully for any inwards
remittances received on behalf of customers / constituents of bank &/or any other debit (inward)
entries unaccounted & liabilities for the bank.
Exempted Categories: While examining DTL, specifically examine following items:
Minimum Eligible Credit (EC) & outstanding Long-Term Bonds (LB) to finance Infrastructure loans &
affordable housing loan.
Eligible amount of incremental FCNR(B) & NRE deposits of maturities of 3 years & above,
Also verify loans out of FCNR(B) deposits & inter- bank Foreign Currency (IBFC) deposits for reporting
n Form A return. Banks should convert forex assets/liabilities (including borrowings) in USD, GBP, JPY
& Euro into INR at RBI reference rate. For other currencies convert into USD using New York rate.
BI Circular on "Maintenance of CRR/SLR on Foreign Currency Assets/Liabilities: Reference rate for
NR/USD & other major currencies",
For conversion of foreign currency Assets/ Liabilities reference rate from FBIL be taken.
- If reference rate not available from FBIL, may use New York closing rate for conversion into USD.
ther Points:
Examine whether consolidations prepared by bank include relevant info of all branches.
Also examine whether balances in Branch Adjustment $a/c$ of foreign branches considered in arriving
at net balance in Branch Adjustment $a/c$.
Examine correctness of data in Form A return for CRR & Form VIII for SLR on sample basis.

Fun
Verify correctness of master data of loan a/cs. Check parameters like instalments, EMI, rate of interest,
tenure of loans etc.
Verify that each bank customer is tagged under single customer id for all it's a/cs.

Examine a/cs adversely commented by concurrent auditors/RBI/bank's internal inspection team.
Examine list of restructured $a/cs$ to ensure restructured as per RBI. They require additionel
provisioning.
Examine quick/early mortality a/cs (Advance slippage to NPA within 12 months of sanction)
Examine all large advances & others on sample basis

Extant guidelines >>>> Proper reporting [Crs & stocks] >>>> Construction  Stock audit
Accounts with temporary deficiencies
Not classify as NPA due to temporary deficiencies such as non-availability of DP based on latest availa
stock statement, o/s balance > limit temporarily & non-renewal of limits on due date.
Stock statements for determining DP shouldn't be older than 3 months.
If DP of a/c calculated from stock statements older than 3 months, it's considered irregular.
Limits not reviewed: Where ad hoc/ regular limit not reviewed within 180 days from due date, consi
as NPA. Also ensure review not done on repetitive basis.
Asset classification → Borrower wise & not facility wise
Ensured that all facilities granted to borrower treated as NPA, if one facility becomes irregular.
Govt Guaranteed Advances [Overdue > 90 days]
Housing wan    
  lean sasdar N   
used
CG guarantee: NPA only if CG repudiates guarantee when invoked. Income recognition on realisation
If CG guarantee not invoked for long, report reasons in LFAR
Note: No exception for SG guarantee i.e, treat as NPA if overdue > 90 days
Agricultural Advance
Agricultural advances are of 2 types:
a. Long Duration Crops (crop season > 1 year) & b. Short Duration Crops (crop season $<=1$ year)
NPA Provisions:
Long Duration Crops: Principal or interest overdue for 1 crop season
Short Duration Crops: Principal or interest overdue for 2 crop seasons
NPA norms as per crop season determined by State Level Bankers' Committee in each State.
Applicable to all direct agricultural advances in Master Circular on lending to priority sector.
For agricultural loans, other than specified in circular, NPAs on same basis as non-agricultural ad
Provisioning Towards Standard Assets
Check the latest RBI Circulars.
Provision for standard assets also required as per RBI norms.
Provisions to be checked in detail with statement of advances.
Bifurcation of standard advances under relevant category should be checked & certified at bra

69 loans90das   whole
Even
 NPA:
CG guarantee: NPA only if CG repudiates guarantee when invoked. Income recognition on realisation basis
Note: No exception for SG guarantee i.e, treat as NPA if overdue > 90 days
Agricultural Advance
Agricultural advances are of 2 types:
a. Long Duration Crops (crop season > 1 year) & b. Short Duration Crops (crop season < $:=1$ year)
NPA Provisions:
Long Duration Crops: Principal or interest overdue for 1 crop season
Short Duration Crops: Principal or interest overdue for 2 crop seasons
NPA norms as per crop season determined by State Level Bankers' Committee in each State.
• Applicable to all direct agricultural advances in Master Circular on lending to priority sector.
0
For agricultural loans, other than specified in circular, NPAs on same basis as non-agricultural advances
Rasd Provisioning Towards Standard Assets
 
Check the latest RBI Circulars.
Provision for standard assets also required as per RBI norms.
0
Provisions to be checked in detail with statement of advances.
 Bifurcation of standard advances under relevant category should be checked & certified at branches.

06a Bank Audit
Restructured Advance
Lec
Fu
Restructuring is act in which lender for economic or legal reasons related to borrower's fina
difficulty, grants concessions to borrower.
 May involve modification of terms of advances including alteration of instalments/repayment period/
of intt./sanction of additional cr. facilities etc.
 Verify compliance with requirements of circular.
Banks may restructure std, substandard or doubtful a/cs. Can't restructure with retrospective eff
Once bank receives application for restructuring, it implies that a/c is intrinsically weak, chec
provision should be made.
Ο On restructuring, $a/c$ will be downgraded from Std to sub-std. NPAs remain in same category.
Upgradation of Account
Examine all accounts upgraded from NPA to std. category during year as per RBI guidelines.
There can be possibility of incorrect upgradation on basis of partial recoveries in the a/c & ove
portion might not have wiped out completely.
Also possibility of recoveries after cut-off date & $a/c$ being upgraded as on BS date.
Auditor shall examine:
Sale/ Purchase of NPAS
BOD Policy for procedures, delegation of power & valuation procedures.
• Only NPA that remained in books for atleast 2 years can be sold.
Sale/purchase without recourse only i.e. entire cr. risk of NPA transferred to purchasing bank
After the sale bank doesn't assume 'any risk'
 NPA sold at cash basis only. Can't be sold at contingent price.
- Bank don't purchase NPA already sold
Spl. Points for Sale of NPA
Removed from books after sale
If Sale Net book value, Shortfall on sale charged to P&L
If Sale > Net book value, excess provision not reversed but retained to meet loss on sale of other
Spl points for purchase of NPA
rovision as per classification status in books of purchaser
ny recovery first adjusted against acquisition cost & excess as profit
or Capital adequacy, 100% risk weight for NPAs purchased from other banks

ify opening balance w.r.t schedule, ledger or fixed assets register.
uisition of new premises verified w.r.t authorisation, title deeds etc.

usiness transactions carried in current $a/c$.

asis, confirmations received.

noperative $A/cs$ (saving/current)
noperative $a/cs$ are high-risk area of frauds. As per RBI, $a/c$ treated as inoperative/dormant
ransactions for period of 2 years.
erify on sample basis some inoperative $a/c$, revived/closed during year.

Opened >> balances >>> interest >>> confirmation >> dr. balance >>> Inoperative $a/c$
Case Study: During audit you notice large no. of cash credit $a/c$ in branch only partially utilized d
ear. However, in March, $a/cs$ are fully utilized. On further scrutiny, observed that these $a/c$ ho
made FDs from utilized amounts at end of year. These deposits liquidated in 1st week of April of
Y. Comment on how this situation would be dealt by you as statutory branch auditor?
Many cash credit $a/c$ are only partially utilized during substantial part of year. However, in March.
re fully utilized. Further, its observed that these $a/c$ holders have made FDs from utilized amour
nd of year. These deposits have been liquidated in 1st week of April of next FY.
This is eg. of window dressing by artificially boosting its advances & deposits.
Regulatory guidelines also prohibit such type of practices & might involve penal action in ter
Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
It needs to be reported in audit report & commented in LFAR also.
Make a suitable qualification in main audit report.

Adequte ICs to ensure transactions are authorised
Test completeness of recorded obligations
Review reasonableness of CL
Verify for Letter of Credits (LCs) for import of goods, payments made in terms of LC
Review whether comfort letters issued by bank included in CL
Guarantee
Claims against bank not acknowledged as debt
Examine relevant evidence, e.g. correspondence with lawyers, workers, officers etc.
Review minutes of meetings of BOD, contracts, agreements etc.
Ascertain from mgt status of claims o/s at year end
Review of subsequent events for evidence about completeness & valuation
diability on Account of Outstanding Forward Exchange Contracts & derivative contracts
Verify with computerised statement generated from a/c system.
May physically verify documents including confirmations to test existence of such contracts.
May verify o/s derivative contracts like options, interest rate swaps etc. with reports..
Guarantees given on behalf of constituents
Check Internal Controls over issue of guarantees
Controls over unused guarantee forms e.g. under custody of responsible officers.
Examine guarantee register for procedure of marking off expired guarantees
Check guarantee register to ensure all included in disclosures
If claim risen, consider if provision required as per AS 29.
Acceptances, Endorsements and Other Obligations
Evaluate ICs over issuance of letters of credit & custody of unused LC forms.
Verify balance of LC from bank's register that indicates amount of LCs & payments under them.
Security for issuing LC should be verified.
Whether bank has potential financial obligation for LC & if yes, disclose under contingent liabilit

Verify outward BFC w.r.t register.

06a Bank Audit

Auditor's Reports
For nationalized bank, report issued to Central Govt. stating following:
Whether BS contains all necessary particulars & exhibit true & fair view of affairs of bank.
Whether P&L a/c shows true balance of profit or loss for period.
If auditor called for any information or explanation, whether it has been given & satisfactory.
Whether or not transactions of bank within powers of bank. (MOA)
Whether or not returns from offices & branches adequate for audit.
Any other matter which should be brought to notice of CG.

Fur
Note: Ensure info. of no. of unaudited branches given & quantification of advances, deposits, intere
income & expense for such unaudited branches also disclosed in audit report.
CARO 2020 is N.A for Banking co.
Also Sec 143 (3) (j) applicable to Co..

Auditor's Reports
For nationalized bank, report issued to Central Govt. stating following:
Whether BS contains all necessary particulars & exhibit true & fair view of affairs of bank.
Whether P&L a/c shows true balance of profit or loss for period.
 
If auditor called for any information or explanation, whether it has been given & satisfactory.
 
Whether or not transactions of bank within powers of bank. (MOA)
Whether or not returns from offices & branches adequate for audit.
Any other matter which should be brought to notice of CG.
Note: Ensure info. of no. of unaudited branches given & quantification of advances, deposits, inter
income & expense for such unaudited branches also disclosed in audit report.
CARO 2020 is N.A for Banking co.

Scope of Concurrent Audit in Banks
06a Bank Aud
Like Internal Audit
Concurrent Audit
Cash + Deposits + Advances + Investments + Foreign Exchange
Coverage of Business/Branches for concurrent Audit
+ House Keeping + Other Items
Admin.
Scope of work to be entrusted to concurrent auditor, coverage etc is discretion of head of Interr
Audit with approval of Audit Committee of Board of Directors (ACB)-
Cash
Areas of focus in Concurrent Audit
Daily cash transactions w.r.t any abnormal/high value receipts & payments.
Proper accounting of inward & outward cash remittances.
Proper accounting of currency chest transactions, prompt reporting to RBI.
Expenses incurred by cash payment involving sizeable amount.
Investments
Purchase/sale of securities should as per: HO instructions & beneficial Rates
Securities in books should be physically held by it
Compliance with RBI/HO guidelines
Deposits
Check transactions about deposits received & repaid.
Check percentage of interest paid on deposits.
Check new current a/cs opened. Operations in new current/Saving a/c for any unusual operations.
Advances
V
Foreign Exchange Tu
heck whether inward/outward remittance properly accounted for.
nsure adherence to guidelines issued by RBI/HO of bank.
heck foreign bills negotiated under letters of credit.
xamine extension & cancellation of forward contracts for purchase & sale of foreign currency.
sure balances in Nostro a/cs in different foreign currencies within prescribed limit.
asure verification/reconciliation of Nostro & Vostro $a/c$ transactions/balances.
eck FCNR & other non-resident a/c whether debits & credits are permissible.

Lea

Option: own staff or external auditor  discretion of bank
Money Transfer
If own official: experienced, trained & senior + independent of branch where audit is conducted
External auditor: ACB decides max. tenure + remuneration. Generally, not be more than 5 years
auditor will continue with a branch/Business unit for more than 3 years.
If omission/commission by auditor, report to RBI & ICAI & appointment maybe cancelled.
Reporting Systems in case of concurrent audit
auf   )
Proper reporting of findings. Each bank should prepare a structured format.
Zone wise reporting & annual report of audit system should be placed before ACB.
Before submission of report, discuss issues with branch manager & concerned officers.
Minor irregularities pointed out by concurrent auditors to be timely rectified. Serious irregulari
reported to controlling offices/ Head Offices.
Whenever fraud detected, immediately report to Inspection & Audit Department (HO) + Chief Vigila
Officer + Branch Managers (unless branch manager involved).
llow-up action by controlling office/Inspection & Audit Department & timely rectification.

NBFC Classification [RBI Scale Based Regulations(SBR)]
Base
NBFC classification based on 4 layers based on their size, activity & riskiness.
Base Layer shall comprise of
a. non-deposit taking NBFCs below asset size of  1000 cr &
b
Middle Layer shall consist of
066 NBFC Au
 . all deposit taking NBFCs (NBFC-Ds), irrespective of asset size,
D. non-deposit taking NBFCs with asset size >= 1000 crore &
 . NBFCs undertaking following activities
(i) Core Investment Companies (CICs)
(iii) Housing Finance Companies (HFCs)
(iv) Infrastructure Debt Fund - NBFCs (IDF-NBF
(ii) Standalone Primary Dealers (SPDs)
(v) Infrastructure Finance Companies (NBFC-IFCs)
Core >>> Standalone >> Housing >>> Infrastructure [Debt fund/finance cos]
Eg. if Housing finance Co. has asset size of 1800 Cr >> covered in middle layer. Asset size doesn't m
Upper Layer: NBFCs specifically identified by RBI based on a set of parameters & scoring methoo
Top 10 eligible NBFCs in terms of asset size always in upper layer, irrespective of any other facte
Top Layer: Ideally empty. Can get populated if RBI is of opinion that there's substantial incre
potential systemic risk in NBFCs in Upper Layer. Such NBFCs shall move to Top from Upper Laye
operations
Categorisation of NBFCs carrying out specific activity
JBFC-P2P, AA, NOFHC & NBFCs w/o public funds & customer interface will always remain in Base
JBFC-D, CIC, IFC & HFC will be included in Middle/Upper Layer (not Base layer). SPD & IDF-NE
ways remain in Middle Layer
emaining NBFCs,
$(covcet\rightarrow Icc\rightarrow MGc_{geiitte~\theta cune(miuefnc/M2))$
vestment & Credit Cos. (NBFC-ICC), Micro Finance Institution (NBFC-MFI), NBFC-Factors &
ortgage Guarantee Companies (NBFC-MGC) could lie in any of layers depending on parameters
vernment owned NBFCs placed in Base/Middle Layer. Not in Upper Layer.
Diff b/w Bank & NBFC
[Am]
FC can't accept Demand Deposits, but some NBFC can accept Term Deposits.
Cs don't form part of payment & settlement system & can't issue cheques drawn on itself
osit insurance facility of DICGC not available to depositors of NBFC unlike banks
minimum exposure to priority sector in case of NBFC

Asset
NBFC Classification [RBI Scale Based Regulations(SBR)]
NBFC classification based on 4 layers based on their size, activity & riskiness.
Base Layer shall comprise of
a. non-deposit taking NBFCs below asset size of  1000 cr &
b. NBFCs undertaking following activities-
NBFC Peer to Peer Lending (NBFC-P2P)
NBFC-Account Aggregator (NBFC-AA)
Middle Layer shall consist of
06b NBFC Audit
Non-Operative Financial Holding Co. (NOFHC)
NBFCs not availing public funds & no customer interface
a. all deposit taking NBFCs (NBFC-Ds), irrespective of asset size,
b. non-deposit taking NBFCs with asset size >= 1000 crore &
c. NBFCs undertaking following activities
CHISI
(v) Infrastructure Finance Companies (NBFC-IFCs)
Core >> Standalone >> Housing >>> Infrastructure [Debt fund/finance cos]
Eg. if Housing finance Co. has asset size of 2800 Cr >> covered in middle layer. Asset size doesn't matter
Upper Layer: NBFCs specifically identified by RBI based on a set of parameters & scoring methodology
Top 10 eligible NBFCs in terms of asset size always in upper layer, irrespective of any other factor.
Top Layer: Ideally empty. Can get populated if RBI is of opinion that there's substantial increase i
potential systemic risk in NBFCs in Upper Layer. Such NBFCs shall move to Top from Upper Layer.
Categorisation of NBFCs carrying out specific activity
a. NBFC-P2P, AA, NOFHC & NBFCs w/o public funds & customer interface will always remain in Base Laye
b. NBFC-D, CIC, IFC & HFC will be included in Middle/Upper Layer (not Base layer). SPD & IDF-NBFC wil
always remain in Middle Layer
c. Remaining NBFCs,
$(covceat\rightarrow Iee\rightarrow MGc_{griiid}\rightarrow civtte~\phi ctns(\mu icAe~fnc(\mu)])$
Investment & Credit Cos. (NBFC-ICC), Micro Finance Institution (NBFC-MFI), NBFC-Factors &
Mortgage Guarantee Companies (NBFC-MGC) could lie in any of layers depending on parameters.
d. Government owned NBFCs placed in Base/Middle Layer. Not in Upper Layer.
Diff b/w Bank & NBFC [4M)
 . NBFC can't accept Demand Deposits, but some NBFC can accept Term Deposits
. NBFCs don't form part of payment & settlement system & can't issue cheques drawn on itself
. Deposit insurance facility of DICGC not available to depositors of NBFC unlike banks
. No minimum exposure to priority sector in case of NBFC

06b NBFC Audit
Capital Adequacy Ratio
Prudential Norms
Learn
With
Fun
Minimimum capital ratio consisting of tier I & II capital not less than 15% of agg. Risk weighted assets
& risk adjusted value of off BS items.
Tier I not less than 10% at any point of time (except NBFC-MFI & lending against gold)
If lending against Gold jewellery (such loans $>=50\%$ of financial assets) → min tier I cap 12%.
"Tier 1 capital" for NBFCs (except NBFCs-BL) is sum of
Owned fund as reduced by investment in shares of other NBFCs & shares, debentures, bonds, $o/s$ loa
& advances, hire purchase & lease finance to & deposits with subsidiaries & group Cos. exceeding,
aggregate, 10% of owned fund &
 Perpetual debt instruments issued by a non-deposit taking NBFCs in each year upto 15% of aggrega
Tier 1 capital of such Co. as on 31 March of previous $a/c$ year.
"Tier 2 capital" for NBFCs (except NBFCs-BL) is sum of
Preference shares other than compulsorily convertible into equity
Revaluation reserves at discounted rate of 55%
General provisions (including for Standard Assets) & loss reserves to the extent not attributable
actual diminution in value or identifiable potential loss in any specific asset & available to me
unexpected losses, to extent of 1.25% of risk weighted assets
Hybrid debt capital instruments
Subordinated debt &
Perpetual debt instruments issued by non-deposit taking NBFC not covered in Tier 1 capital
to the extent aggregate of Tier 2 does not exceed Tier 1 capital
Note: NBFCs-BL are not eligible to include perpetual debt instruments in Tier 1 & 2 capital.
Bose Layer
Risk Weights

i) Owned fund as reduced by investment in shares of other NBFCs & shares, debentures, bonds, $o/s$ loar

) Preference shares other than compulsorily convertible into equity

 ) Hybrid debt capital instruments

) Perpetual debt instruments issued by non-deposit taking NBFC not covered in Tier 1 capital

NBFC allowed to present items of BS in order of liquidity.
NBFC to disclose by way of note item of 'other income/expense' > 1% of total income. Division II require

NBFC separately disclose under receivables, debt due from LLP where Director is partner/member

Eg. AF Ltd. is NBFC & accepting public deposits & giving loans since 2015. Having net owned funds of  1.
Cr & not having registration certificate from RBI & applied on 30.03.25.
Advise auditor w.r.t auditor procedures & reporting requirements of CARO 2020?
As per Clause (xvi) of Para 3 of CARO 2020, report that "whether Co. is required to be regd. u/s 45-I.
of RBI Act, 1934 & if so, whether registration obtained." Examine whether Co. is engaged in busines
which requires registration. Registration is required where financing activity is a principal business o
Co. RBI restrict Cos. from carrying on business of NBFI without certificate of registration.

xamine transactions of Co. w.r.t activities covered under RBI Act & directions.

nancial assets > 50% of gross income.
Whether Co. has net owned funds as required for registration as NBFC.
Whether Co. has obtained registration as NBFC, if not, reasons should be sought & documented.
uditor report incorporating following:-
