Eg. interim financial info. may relate to FS of a quarter of FY.
Agreeing the Terms of Engagement
Auditor & client should agree on terms of engg. Agreed terms of engg. are recorded in engg. letter.
Helps to avoid misunderstandings regarding nature of engg &, in particular, objective & scope of review,
mgt's responsibilities, auditor's responsibilities, assurance obtained, & nature & form of report.
Understanding Entity & its Environment including its Internal Control
Have understanding of entity & its environment, including IC, related to preparation of both annual &
interim financial info., to plan & conduct the engagement to:
a. Identify the types of potential MM & consider likelihood of their occurrence &
b. Select inquiries, analytical and other review procedures that will provide a basis for reporting.
Procedures performed to update understanding of entity & its environment, including its IC, include:
 Reading documentation of PY's audit & reviews of prior interim period(s) of CY & corresponding interim
period(s) of PY, to identify matters that may affect current-period interim financial info.
 Reading most recent annual & comparable prior period interim financial info.
• Consider significant risks, including risk of mgt override of controls, identified in audit of PY's FS.
 Consider nature of any corrected MMs & any identified uncorrected MMs in PY's FS.
 Consider significant accounting & reporting matters of continuing significance such as material
weaknesses in IC.
 Consider materiality to determine nature & extent of procedures & evaluating effect of misstatements.
 Consider results of any audit procedures performed w.r.t current year's FS.
 Considering results of any internal audit performed & subsequent actions taken by mgt.
Learn
With
Fun
SRE 2410 Review of Interim Financial Info. performed by Independent Auditor of Entity
11 S.
SRE 2410 applies when review of interim financial info. is performed by independent auditor of FS
entity.
Interim financial info. is financial info. prepared & presented as per AFRF & comprises either a complet
or condensed set of FS for a period shorter than entity's FY.
Eg. interim financial info. may relate to FS of a quarter of FY.
Agreeing the Terms of Engagement
Auditor & client should agree on terms of engg. Agreed terms of engg. are recorded in engg. letter.
Helps to avoid misunderstandings regarding nature of engg &, in particular, objective & scope of review
mgt's responsibilities, auditor's responsibilities, assurance obtained, & nature & form of report.
Understanding Entity & its Environment including its Internal Control
Have understanding of entity & its environment, including IC, related to preparation of both annual
Interim financial info., to plan & conduct the engagement to:
Identify the types of potential MM & consider likelihood of their occurrence &
Select inquiries, analytical and other review procedures that will provide a basis for reporting.
Procedures performed to update understanding of entity & its environment, including its IC, include:
Reading documentation of PY's audit & reviews of prior interim period(s) of CY & corresponding interir
eriod(s) of PY, to identify matters that may affect current-period interim financial info.
Reading most recent annual & comparable prior period interim financial info.
Consider significant risks, including risk of mgt override of controls, identified in audit of PY's FS.
Consider nature of any corrected MMs & any identified uncorrected MMs in PY's FS,
onsider significant accounting & reporting matters of continuing significance such as materic
eaknesses in IC.
onsider materiality to determine nature & extent of procedures & evaluating effect of misstatements
onsider results of any audit procedures performed w.r.t current year's FS.
onsidering results of any internal audit performed & subsequent actions taken by mgt.

quiring mgt about effect of changes in entity's business activities.
quiring mgt about significant changes in IC & its effect on preparation of interim financial info.
quiring mgt of process by which interim financial info. prepared.

Going Concern
Inquire whether mat has changed its GC assessment.
II SRE
When as a result of inquiry or other review procedures, auditor becomes aware of events or conditions
that may cast significant doubt on entity's ability to continue as a GC:
Inquire of mgt as to its plans for future actions, feasibility of these plans, & whether mgt believes that
outcome of these plans will improve the situation &
Consider adequacy of disclosure about such matters in interim financial info.
How to know abt events?
Events or conditions which may cast significant doubt on GC may have existed at date of annual FS or
may be identified as result of mgt inquiries or in course of performing other review procedures.

Plans could be to liquidate assets, borrow money or restructure debt, reduce or delay expenditures, or
increase capitał.
Not necessary to corroborate feasibility of mgt's plans & whether outcome of these plans will improve
the situation.
Doubt about accounting treatment
When a matter leads to question whether a material adjustment should be made for interim financial
info. to be prepared, in all material respects, as per AFRF, make additional inquiries or perform other
procedures.
For eg, if auditor's review procedures lead auditor to question whether a significant sales transaction is
recorded as per AFRF, perform additional procedures, such as discussing terms of transaction with
senior marketing & accounting personnel, or reading sales contract.
Evaluation of Misstatements
Evaluate whether uncorrected misstatements are material (individually & in aggregate).
Exercises PJ in evaluating materiality of any misstatements that entity has not corrected.

uch items is included or attached to WR.

 has disclosed to auditor all significant subsequent events that may require adjustment or disclosure

II SRE
Learn
With
Fun
720
Auditor's Responsibility for Accompanying Info.
Read other info. that accompanies interim financial info. to consider whether it's materially inconsistent
with interim financial info.
If auditor identifies a material inconsistency, consider whether interim financial info. or other info
needs to be amended.
If amendment necessary in interim financial info. & mgt refuses, considers implications for review report
If amendment necessary in other info. & mgt refuses, considers including in review report additional para
describing material inconsistency or taking other actions, such as withholding issuance of review report
or withdrawing from engg.
Example of Material Inconsistency?
Mgt may present alternative measures of earnings that more positively portray results of operations
than interim financial info, & such alternative measures are not clearly defined or reconciled to interim
financial info. such that they are confusing & potentially misleading.
Losses
Q. What if auditor identified material misstatement of FACT in other info?
Discuss matter with mgt.
When discussing with mgt, consider validity of other info. & mgt's responses to inquiries, whether valid
differences of judgment or opinion exist & whether to request mgt to consult with qualified 3rd party to
resolve apparent misstatement of fact.
If amendment is necessary & mgt refuses, consider appropriate further action, like notifying TCWG &
obtaining a legal advice.
 Communication
When auditor believes material adjustment is necessary to interim financial info., communicate to mgt.
When mgt doesn't timely respond, inform TCWG.
Communication is made, either orally or in writing.
Decision to communicate orally or inwriting is affected by factors such as nature, sensitivity &
ignificance of matter & timing of communications. If communicated orally, document the communication.
When, TCWG don't respond timely, consider:
- Whether to modify the report or
- Possibility of withdrawing from engagement &
Possibility of resigning from appointment to audit the annual FS.
When auditor believes in existence of fraud/NOCLAR, communicate to Mgt.
Iso report such matters to TCWG & considers implication for review.
atters that in auditor's opinion important & relevant to TCWG in overseeing financial reporting å
sclosure process. Communicates such matters to TCWG.

II SRE
Limitation on Scope
Learn
W
Fun
When auditor unable to complete review, communicate, in writing, to mgt & TCWG reason why revien
can't be completed & consider whether its appropriate to issue a report.
Limitation on Scope Imposed by Mgt
Don't accept engg, if auditor would be unable to complete review because of limitation on scope by mgt.
If, after accepting engg, mgt imposes a limitation on scope, requests removal of that limitation.
If mgt refuses, auditor is unable to complete review & express a conclusion.
Communicates, in writing, to mgt & TCWG reason why review cannot be completed.
Also considers L/R responsibilities, including whether there is requirement to issue report.
If yes, auditor disclaims a conclusion & provides reason why review can't be completed.
Other Limitations on Scope
Rarely limitation is confined to specific matters that are material, but not pervasive.
In such circumstances, modify review report by indicating that, except for matter described in
explanatory para, to review report, review was conducted as per this SRE, & qualify the conclusion.
Auditor may have expressed qualified opinion on audit of latest annual FS because of limitation on scope.
Consider whether that limitation still exists &, if so, implications for review report.
Going Concern and Significant Uncertainties
Material uncertainty (MU) relating to event or condition that may cast significant doubt on entity's
bility to continue as GC & adequate disclosure is made in interim financial info, auditor modifies review
eport by adding an EOM para. MURG

f MU still exists & adequate disclosure is made, modify the review report on current interim financial
fo. by adding a para to highlight continued MU.
MU not adequately disclosed, express qualified or adverse conclusion, as appropriate.
eport should include specific reference to fact that there is such a MU...
port with Interim Financial Info.

en reviewed by entity's auditor, review report will also be included in document.
mgt has not included review report in document, seek legal advice to determine appropriate course of
-ion.
auditor issued modified review report & mgt issues interim financial info, without including modified
iew report in document containing interim financial info, seek legal advice to determine appropriate
rse of action & possibility of resigning from audit of annual FS.

Assurance engg means engg.
in which practitioner expresses a conclusion
to enhance degree of confidence
of intended users other than responsible party
about the outcome of evaluation of subject matter against criteria.
Assurance engg. may relate to examination of subject matters other than FS prepared on basis of
"historical financial info".
May relate to prospective financial info, or non-financial matters like design & operation of internal
Control in an entity.
Level of assurance provided by these Standards on Assurance Engg. is moderate.

SAE 3400 - Examination of Prospective Financial Info. (PFI)
Examine & report on PFI including examination procedures for best-estimate & hypothetical assump
Note:
Need not necessarily be performed by stat. auditor of entity's FS.
This SAE does not apply to examination of PFI expressed in general/narrative terms, such as
discussion & analysis in annual report.
What is Prospective financial information (PFI)?
FI is financial info based on:
ssumptions about events that may occur in the future &
ossible actions by entity.
ospective financial info can be in form of forecast, projection, or combination of both.
9. 1-year forecast plus 5-year projection.
That is Forecast?
FI prepared on the basis of assumptions of
ture events which mgt expects &
tions mgt expects to take on date PFI is prepared.
Est estimate assumptions- assumption that reflects anticipated experience with no provision fo
adverse deviation.
In present market conditions, supply availability, historical buying patterns & seasonal trends
pects sales to increase by 5% in next quarter. Therefore, 5% sales increase is his financial fore
12.1
.
Learn
With
Fun
What is Projection?
PFI prepared on basis of:
Hypothetical assumptions about
☑ future events & mgt actions
☑
not necessarily expected to take place
Eg entities in start-up phase or considering major change in nature of operations or
• Mixture of best-estimate & hypothetical assumptions (imagined or suggested)
Examples:
12 S
1. X Ltd may project a course of action to take when one or more hypothetical situations arise, such
creating a new product to meet demand of expected market growth.
2. Co. plans to raise funds from a prospective equity investor & has asked a firm of CAs to examine prot
forecast for placing it before prospective equity investor.
3. Co. is in process of setting up new plant. Needs loan from bank. Prepared a projection of its profi
cash flows for next 7 years along with assumptions.
Nature of Assurance Regarding PFI
PFI relates to events & actions that have not occurred & might not occur. While evidence may be availab
to support assumptions, such evidence is generally future- oriented & speculative. Therefore, opinion
to whether results shown in PFI will be achieved cannot be expressed.
Mgt Responsibility of Preparation & Presentation of PFI
Identification & disclosure of PFI RAS Basis of brecast: Asseup s
Explaining basis of forecast
Underlying assumptions
Can Professional Accountants be associated with PFI?
Clause 3 of Part I of Second Schedule to CA Act, 1949 states CAIP shall be deemed to be guilty of Pre
Misconduct, if he permits his or firm's name to be used in connection with EOE contingent upon futu
transactions in a manner which may lead to belief that he vouches for accuracy of forecast.
CA can prepare profit/financial forecasts & review them, clearly indicating in report:
Sources of info, Basis of forecasts & major Assumptions & doesn't vouch for accuracy of forecasts.
Also applies to projections made on basis of hypothetical assumptions about future events & mgt actio
not necessarily expected to take place so long as vouching for accuracy isn't made.
Duties of Member Examining PFI
Dup
Auditor should obtain SAME as to whether:
. Mgt's best-estimate assumptions are not unreasonable & hypothetical assumptions are consistent wi
purpose of info.

12 SAE
PFI is properly prepared on basis of assumptions
Learn
With
Fun
PFI is properly presented & all material assumptions adequately disclosed, including clear indication
whether they are best-estimate or hypothetical assumptions &
PFI is prepared on consistent basis with historical FS, using appropriate a/c principles.
Nature of Assurance regarding PFI
PFI relates to events & actions that have not occurred & might not occur. While evidence may be available
to support assumptions, such evidence is generally future- oriented & speculative,
Therefore, opinion as to whether results shown in PFI will be achieved cannot be expressed.
If Precautions to be taken by Auditor Before Accepting such an Engg
Before accepting an engg to examine PFI, auditor would consider, amongst other things:
Intended use of the info.
Whether the info, will be for general or limited distribution
Nature of assumptions, i.e, whether they are best estimates or hypothetical Assumptions
Elements to be included in the info. &
Period covered by the info.
Notes:
Conditions Decline/Withdraw from engg?
Not accept or withdraw from engg, when assumptions are clearly unrealistic or auditor believes that PFI
will be inappropriate for its intended use, Consider extent to which reliance on entity's historical financial
info, is justified.
Steps to formalise terms of engg?
To formalize terms of engg, agree on terms with client by sending an engg. letter, like in other engg.
Examination Procedures
When determining NTE of examination procedures, following matters should be considered: -
Enowledge obtained during any previous engg
Agt's competence regarding preparation of PFI
Likelihood of Material Misstatement
xtent to which PFI is affected by Mgt's judgment
ources of info, considered by mgt for the purpose
tability of entity's business &
ngg team's experience with business & industry in which entity operates & reporting on PFI

-esentation of PFI is informative, & not misleading
 c policies clearly disclosed in notes to PFI

Matters important in providing evidence to support report on examination of PFI, & evidence that su
examination was carried out as per this SAE should be documented.
SAE 3402 Assurance Reports on Controls at a Service Organisation (SO)
Scope of SAE 3402
Applies only when SO responsible for or able to make an assertion about, suitable design of contro
It does not deal with assurance engg:
. To report only on whether controls at SO operated as described or
. To report only on controls at SO other than related to a service relevant to user entities' IC as it relat
to financial reporting (for eg, controls that affect user entities' production or quality control).
Objectives of Service Auditor
To obtain reasonable assurance (RA) whether, in all material respects, based on suitable criteria: -
i) SO's description of system fairly presents system as designed & implemented throughout specifi
period (or in case of type 1 report, as at specified date)
ii) Controls related to control objectives stated in SO's description of system suitably designe
throughout specified period (or in case of type 1 report, as at specified date)
iii) Where included in scope, controls operated effectively to provide RA that control objectives we
achieved throughout specified period.
To Report on matters in (a) above as per service auditor's findings.

SAE 3402 Assurance Reports on Controls at a Service Organisation (SO)
Scope of SAE 3402
Applies only when SO responsible for or able to make an assertion about, suitable design of control
It does not deal with assurance engg:
To report only on whether controls at SO operated as described or
To report only on controls at SO other than related to a service relevant to user entities' IC as it relat
to financial reporting (for eg, controls that affect user entities' production or quality control).
Objectives of Service Auditor
To obtain reasonable assurance (RA) whether, in all material respects, based on suitable criteria: -
i) SO's description of system fairly presents system as designed & implemented throughout specifie
period (or in case of type 1 report, as at specified date)
ii) Controls related to control objectives stated in SO's description of system suitably designe
throughout specified period (or in case of type 1 report, as at specified date)
iii) Where included in scope, controls operated effectively to provide RA that control objectives wer
achieved throughout specified period.
To Report on matters in (a) above as per service auditor's findings.

10. Understanding internal audit function: Understand responsibilities of internal audit function & activities
performed to determine whether internal audit function is likely to be relevant for service auditor to
use specific work of internal auditors.
11 Asking for Written Representations: Service auditor shall request SO to provide WRs:-
a. That reaffirm assertion about description of system
b. That it has provided service auditor with all relevant info. & access &
c. That it has disclosed to service auditor any of the following of which it is aware:-
i) NOCLAR, fraud, or uncorrected deviations in SO that may affect 1 or more user entities
ii) Design deficiencies in controls
iii) Instances where controls have not operated as described &
iv) Any events subsequent to period covered by SO's description of system up to date of service
auditor's assurance report that could have a significant effect on assurance report.
12. Subsequent Events: Inquire whether SO aware of events subsequent to period covered by SO's
description of system up to date of service auditor's assurance report that could have a significant
effect on service auditor's assurance report.
Include the following basic elements: -
Reporting
a. Title that clearly indicates report is an independent service auditor's assurance report.
b. An addressee.
c. Identification of:
i. SO's description of system, & SO's assertion, which includes matters for type 2 or type 1 report.
ii. Parts of description of system not covered by service auditor's opinion.

What is Pro forma financial information (PFFI)?
FI refers to financial info. shown with adjustments to illustrate
☑
✓ impact of event or transaction

notred

bilities & earnings of Issuer. Also include notes for significant aspects of transactions, assumptions &
justments made.
Soch Not actual
is is achieved by applying pro forma adjustments to unadjusted financial info. PFFI does not represent
-ity's actual financial position, performance or cash flows.

obtain RA whether PPFI has been compiled, in all material respects, by responsible party as per
Hicable criteria &
report in accordance with practitioner's findings.

tifying source of unadjusted financial info. & extracting unadjusted financial info. from that source
ng pro forma adjustments to unadjusted financial info. for purpose for which PPFI is presented d
enting resulting PFFI with accompanying disclosures..

SAE 3420 Assurance Engagements to Report on
Compilation of Pro Forma Financial Information (PFFI) Included in a Prospectus
What is Pro forma financial information (PFFI)?
PFFI refers to financial info. shown with adjustments to illustrate
☑ impact of event or transaction
notred
☑ on unadjusted financial info as if event had occurred or transaction undertaken at earlier da
PFFI is used in offer documents.
PFFI may take form of P&L & Balance Sheet to show how transactions might have affected ass
liabilities & earnings of Issuer. Also include notes for significant aspects of transactions, assumption
adjustments made.
Soch Not alle
This is achieved by applying pro forma adjustments to unadjusted financial info. PFFI does not repres
entity's actual financial position, performance or cash flows.
Objectives
To obtain RA whether PPFI has been compiled, in all material respects, by responsible party as
applicable criteria &
To report in accordance with practitioner's findings.
 Steps in compilation of PFFI
Identifying source of unadjusted financial info. & extracting unadjusted financial info. from that so
Making pro forma adjustments to unadjusted financial info. for purpose for which PPFI is presented
Presenting resulting PFFI with accompanying disclosures..
Identify source + extract >> Adjustments >>> Present & disclose
12.9
Learn
With
Fun
Nature of Practitioner's Responsibility
12 SAE
No responsibility to compile PFFI for entity. Such responsibility rests with responsible party.
Sole responsibility is to report whether PFFI compiled, in all material respects, as per applicable criteria.
It's a RA engg to report on compilation of PFFI involving performing procedures to assess whether
applicable criteria used in compilation of PFFI provide a reasonable basis for presenting significant
effects of event or transaction.
1. Engagement Acceptance
Steps involved in such Engagement
a. Determine that practitioner has competence & capabilities to perform the engg
b. On basis of preliminary knowledge & discussion with responsible party, determine that applicable criteria
are suitable & unlikely that PFFI will be misleading for intended purpose.
c. Evaluate wording of opinion to determine that practitioner will be able to express opinion based on
procedures specified in this SAE
d. Where sources from which unadjusted financial info been extracted been audited/reviewed & modified
audit opinion/review conclusion been expressed or report contains EOM para, consider whether relevant
L/R permits use or reference in practitioner's report to, modified audit opinion or review conclusion or
report containing EOM para w.r.t such sources
e. If entity's historical financial info. never been audited/reviewed, consider whether sufficient
understanding obtained of entity & accounting & financial reporting practices to perform engg.
f. If event/transaction includes acquisition & acquiree's historical financial info. never been
audited/reviewed, consider whether sufficient understanding be obtained of acquiree & its accounting
& financial reporting practices to perform engg &
. Obtain agreement of responsible party that it acknowledges & understands its responsibility for:
i) Adequately disclosing & describing applicable criteria to intended users if not publicly available
ii) Compiling PFFI on basis of applicable criteria &
iii) Providing practitioner with:-
a. Access to all relevant info. (including, info. of acquiree(s) in a business combination)
b. Additional info, that practitioner may request from responsible party
c. Access to those within entity & entity's advisors from whom practitioner determines necessary
to obtain evidence &
d. When needed for engg purposes, access to appropriate individuals within acquiree(s) in a
business combination.
Planning & Performing the Engg.
Assess whether applicable criteria are suitable.
Materiality for evaluating whether PFFI compiled in all material respects on basis of applicable criteria.
Understanding of how responsible party compiled PFFI & other engg. circumstances
Obtain evidence about appropriateness of source from which unadjusted financial info extracted

If no audit or review report on source from which unadjusted financial info extracted, pe

Determine whether responsible party appropriately extracted unadjusted financial info from sour
 Obtain evidence about appropriateness of pro forma adjustments.
In relation to unadjusted financial info, Pro forma adjustments include:

Written Representations from Responsible Party
a. Identified all appropriate pro forma adjustments to illustrate impact of event or transaction &
b. PFFI has been compiled, in all material respects, on the basis of applicable criteria.
Opinion
Unmodified Opinion: When practitioner concludes that PFFI has been compiled, in all material resp
on the basis of applicable criteria.
Modified Opinion
a. Prospectus Not Permitted: Where L/R prevents publication of prospectus that contains a mod
opinion w.r.t whether PFFI been compiled, in all material respects, on basis of applicable criter
practitioner concludes modified opinion is appropriate, discuss the matter with responsible party.
If responsible party doesn't agree to make necessary changes:
Withdraw from engg or Consider seeking legal advice.
b. Prospectus Permitted: Where L/R allows publication of prospectus that contains modified opinion
PFFI & practitioner determines that modified opinion is appropriate, apply requirements in Frame
for Assurance Engg regarding modified opinions.
Emphasis of Matter Para
 May draw user's attention to a matter presented/disclosed in PFFI or notes.
Matter is fundamental to user's understanding of whether PFFI been compiled, in all material resp
on basis of applicable criteria.
 Provided practitioner obtained SAAE about the matter.
Such para refer only to info. presented or disclosed in PFFI or notes.

SRS
Standards on Related Services (non-assurance engagements)
SRS 4400 Engagements to Perform Agreed-upon Procedures Regarding Financial Information
SRS 4410 Compilation Engagements
Not all engagements performed by practitioners are assurance engagements.
Other frequently performed engg. that are not assurance engg. include:-
Preparation of tax returns where no assurance is expressed.
Consulting (or advisory) engg. such as mgt & tax consulting.

Fun
بازها
Engg. covered by SRS, such as agreed-upon procedures engg. & compilations of financial or other info.
What are Agreed-upon Procedures?
Auditor is engaged to issue a report of factual findings, based on
specified procedures performed
on specified subject matter
of specified elements, $a/cs$ or items of a FS.
Eg. Perform certain procedures on individual items of FS, say, $a/c$ payable, $a/c$ receivable, RP purchases
sales & profits of a segment, or a FS, say, a BS or even complete set of FS,
However, person performing related services need not necessarily be auditor of entity's FS.
SRS 4400 Engg. to Perform Agreed Upon Procedure regarding Financial Info.
Objective
arry out procedures of audit nature to which auditor & entity & any appropriate 3rd parties have agreed
report on factual findings.
assurance is provided.
ers of report assess procedures & findings & draw their own conclusions.
port is usually restricted to parties that have agreed to procedures to be performed since others,
ware of reasons for procedures, may misinterpret the results.
mmary: No assurance is provided in such a report. These are non-assurance engg.
Audit Vs. Agreed-upon Procedures (AUP)
difference between audit & AUP relates to assurance.
It expresses opinion & provides assurance to users.
ever, in AUP, only report of factual findings provided. No assurance is given to users.
ead, users draw own conclusions based on factual findings in report.
eg, AUP engg. to evaluate validity of $a/c$ payable may state following:
Comparing names of major suppliers & $o/s$ amounts as on date to names & amounts in trial balance.

Standards on Related Services (non-assurance engagements)
☑
SRS 4400 Engagements to Perform Agreed-upon Procedures Regarding Financial Information
SRS 4410 Compilation Engagements
Not all engagements performed by practitioners are assurance engagements.
Other frequently performed engg. that are not assurance engg. include:-
Preparation of tax returns where no assurance is expressed.
Consulting (or advisory) engg. such as mgt & tax consulting.
Engg. covered by SRS, such as agreed-upon procedures engg. & compilations of financial or other int
What are Agreed-upon Procedures?
Auditor is engaged to issue a report of factual findings, based on
specified procedures performed
on specified subject matter
of specified elements, a/cs or items of a FS.
Eg. Perform certain procedures on individual items of FS, say, a/c payable, a/c receivable, RP purcha
& sales & profits of a segment, or a FS, say, a BS or even complete set of FS,
of However, person performing related services need not necessarily be auditor of entity's FS.
SRS 4400 Engg. to Perform Agreed Upon Procedure regarding Financial Info.
Objective
Carry out procedures of audit nature to which auditor & entity & any appropriate 3rd parties have agr
& report on factual findings.
No assurance is provided.
Users of report assess procedures & findings & draw their own conclusions.
Report is usually restricted to parties that have agreed to procedures to be performed since othe
unaware of reasons for procedures, may misinterpret the results.
Summary: No assurance is provided in such a report. These are non-assurance engg.
Audit Vs. Agreed-upon Procedures (AUP)
Key difference between audit & AUP relates to assurance.
Audit expresses opinion & provides assurance to users.
However, in AUP, only report of factual findings provided. No assurance is given to users.
Instead, users draw own conclusions based on factual findings in report.
For eg, AUP engg. to evaluate validity of $a/c$ payable may state following:
 Comparing names of major suppliers & o/s amounts as on date to names & amounts in trial balanc

Obtaining suppliers statements/confirmations from suppliers to confirm $o/s$ balances. Comparis
of such statements/confirmations to amounts in trial balance.
Actual findings like variation in balances reflected in trial balance & statements or confirmatio
are given.
Actual findings are reported as such without providing an assurance.
Q. Is Auditor required to be independent for such agreed upon Procedures? No but should comply wi
other ethical requirements. Terms of engg. may require to comply with independence.
Terms of Engagement
Matters to be agreed include the following:
Nature of engg including the fact that procedures performed will not constitute an audit or review
accordingly no assurance will be expressed.
Stated Purpose for engg.
- Identification of financial info, to which agreed-upon procedures will be applied.
.NTE of specific procedures to be applied.
- Limitations on distribution of report of factual findings. When such limitation is in conflict with le
requirements, if any, auditor would not accept engg.
Matters that would be included in engagement letter include:
Listing of procedures to performed as agreed-upon between the parties.
Statement that distribution of report of factual findings would be restricted to specified parties w
have agreed to the procedures.
Planning: Plan the work for an effective engg.
Procedures
Procedures applied in an engagement to perform agreed-upon procedures may include:
Inquiry and analysis
Observation
Inspection
Obtaining confirmations
Re computation, comparison and other clerical accuracy checks
Reporting [elements]
. Title
-. Addressee (ordinarily, the appointing authority)
. Identification of specific financial/non-financial info. to which agreed upon procedures applied
Statement that procedures performed were those agreed-upon with recipient
 . Statement that engg. was performed as per SRS
. Identification of purpose
1.Listing of specific procedures performed

SRS 4410 Compilation Engg.
Assist mgt with preparation & presentation of historical financial info. without any assurance on tha
info, & report on engg. as per this SRS.
 Applies to compilation engg. for historical financial info.
- Compilation engg. for other info. can be performed under this Std after necessary adaptation.
SQC 1 applies to compilation engg. too.
Practitioner need not be independent but needs to comply with ethical requirements.
Remember, compilation engg. isn't assurance engg. hence no expression of opinion.
Why Compilation Engg required?
Financial info. that's subject of a compilation engg. may be required for various purposes: -
To comply with mandatory periodic FR requirements established in $L/R$
For purposes unrelated to mandatory FR under $L/R,$ for eg:
or
For mgt or TCWG, prepared on a basis appropriate for their particular purposes eg. for internal us

/financing structure (merger or acquisition)

based on info. provided by mgt &
Report as per requirements of this SRS.

Intended use & distribution of financial info & any restrictions on its use or distribution

SRS 4410 Compilation Engg.
Assist mgt with preparation & presentation of historical financial info. without any assurance on t
info, & report on engg. as per this SRS.
Applies to compilation engg. for historical financial info.
Compilation engg. for other info. can be performed under this Std after necessary adaptation.
• SQC 1 applies to compilation engg. too.
Practitioner need not be independent but needs to comply with ethical requirements.
Remember, compilation engg. isn't assurance engg. hence no expression of opinion.
Why Compilation Engg required?

 To comply with mandatory periodic FR requirements established in $L/R$ or

/financing structure (merger or acquisition)

CHAPTER
14
SDG & ESG Assurance
Lear
With
Fun
Sustainability reporting & its expected benefits
 Practice of reporting publicly economic, environmental, and/or social impacts & contributions (positive
or negative), towards sustainable development.
Info that companies provide about performance to outside world on regular basis in structured way.
Expected Benefits: ULI
 Understand performance: Help stakeholders to understand organizations performance vis a vis
sustainability & impacts. Reporting process links financial & non-financial performance.
 Long term value: Such reporting can help entities to focus on long-term value creation, by addressing
environmental, social and governance (ESG) issues.
 Investing: Investors are increasingly recognising environmental & social issues provide both risks &
opportunities in respect of investments & seeking disclosures on environmental & social performance of
businesses, they can use ESG performance to make investment decisions.
Three pillars constitute ESG
A. Environment (E): Environmental stands for corporate climate policies, energy use, waste, pollutions,
natural resource conservation & treatment of animals. Includes natural resources that every entity
uses like coal, water etc.
B. Social (S): Addresses relationships & reputation with people & institutions. Also includes labour
relations, diversity & inclusions.
C. Governance (G): It is ICs & procedures entity adopts to govern, make effective investment decisions,
comply with law & meet needs of stakeholders.
ESG Reporting:
ESG reporting disclose info, data, metrics that explain value added in these 3 areas.
It can be both quantitative & qualitative in nature.
 Qualitative reports describe Co's strategy or policy & Quantitative approach includes metrics & KPIS
to measure progress against goals & report achievements.
O
Mixed approach that uses both qualitative & quantitative info adds to quality of disclosures.
17 Sustainable Development Goals [Quick Read]
No Poverty | Zero Hunger | Good health & well being | Quality Education | Gender Equality | Clear
Water & Sanitization | Affordable & Clean Energy | Decent Work & Economic Growth | Industry
Innovation & Infrastructure | Reduced Inequalities | Sustainable Cities & Communities | Responsible
Consumption & Production | Climate Action | Life Below Water | Life on Land | Peace | Justice & Strong
Institutions | Partnership for the Goals
Note: Corporates can contribute to SDGs due to their capacity to provide solutions for meeting SDGS
Cos. can lead in innovation & contribute to achievement of SDGS.
14.1

Integrated Reporting (IR)
14 SDG & ESG ASS
SHIMNA
Financials
6 Cs of Integrated Reporting - also known as 6 capitals:
(i) Financial Capital: Pool of funds available for producing goods or services. Obtained via financing,
Eg debt, equity or grants or generated through operations or investments.
(ii) Manufactured Capital: Human-created, production equipment & tools. Available for production of
or services.
(iii) Natural Capital: Input to production of goods/services. Org's activities also impact natural capita
Eg. water, land, minerals & forests, biodiversity, etc.
(iv) Human Capital: People's skills & experience, capacity & motivations, including their:
☑ Alignment with org's governance & ethical values.
☑
☑
Ability to understand & implement org's strategy.
Loyalties & motivations for improving processes, goods & services
(v) Social Capital: Institutions & relationships established within & between each community, grou
stakeholders & other networks to enhance individual & collective well-being.
Includes:
☑
☑
☑
Common values & behaviour.
key relationships, trust & loyalty developed with customers, suppliers & business partners.
social license to operate.
(vi) Intellectual Capital: Key element in org's future earning potential, investment in R&D, innovation, H
external relationships, which can determine organization's competitive advantage.
Note: IR voluntary for top 500 listed cos.
Inf Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report (BRSR)
Top 1000 listed companies by market capitalization required to file BRSR.
Reporting questionnaire is divided into 3 sections:
i) Section A - General Disclosures
Details of listed cos., products, services, operations, employee details, holding, subsidiary, associate
etc.
ii) Section B - Management Process and disclosures
Questions related to policy & mgt processes, governance, leadership, & oversight.
iii) Section C - Principle-wise performance disclosures
Report upon Key performance indicators (KPIs) in alignment with 9 principles of NGRBC
The section classifies KPIs into 2 categories that companies are required to report upon:
ad - Essential indicators (Mandatory disclosures)
[National Guidelines on Responsible Business Condu
Data on training programs, environmental data on energy, emissions, water, waste mgt etc.
Leadership indicators (Optional disclosures)
Life cycle assessments, conflict mgt policy, additional data on biodiversity, energy consumptions, sup
chain mgt etc.

Reporting questionnaire is divided into 3 sections:

14.2
14 SDG & ESG Assurance
Nine Principles of BRSR
Learn
Fun
Jup
9 principles in BRSR are categorized into ESG components of Environment, Social & Governance with 2
in Environment, 3 in social & 4 in Governance.
For eg, environmental protection is closely linked to stakeholder engagement & inclusive growth.
Principle 1 - Ethics, Transparency & Accountability (EAT),
Emphasizes that business decisions should be open to disclosure & accessible to relevant parties.
Core elements:
Policies: Entities' governing structure should develop policies, procedures & practices for their offices,
factories & work areas, ensuring ethics is not compromised.
Share with stakeholders: Info. of policies, procedures & practices with performance should be made
available to stakeholders.
galli
Adverse effects: For adverse effects, care has to be taken for transparent disclosures.
. Encourage others: Entities in value chain should be encouraged to adopt these principles.
-. Proactively respond: Entities should proactively respond to outside entities that violate 9 principles.
This includes suppliers, distributors, etc that engage with business concern.
Principle 2 - Safe & Sustainable Goals & Service
Make sure that goods, services & operations result in better life for consumers & end-users.
25 Core elements:.
. Minimise Resources: Product design, production methods & technologies should minimize resource usage
. Spread Awareness: Entities to educate & make consumers & clients aware about their rights.
. RRR: Take measures that reduce over exploitation of nature's resources by consuming sustainably &
encourage methods for reduce, reuse & recycling of resources.
Principle 3 - Promote well-being of all employees including those in value chain
Initiatives entity takes for benefit of its employees dignity, health, well-being.
Core elements:
Pension
Bonus Grabuiky.
■. Compliance: Ensure compliance with all regulatory, requirements as far as employees are concerned,
2. Dignity & Freedom: Respect dignity of employee as human being & shouldn't restrict their freedom of
associations, unions for collective bargaining of rights & redressal of issues.
. Child Labour: Prevent child labour, bonded labour & other forms of involuntary labour.
d. Work life Balance: Have system in which work-life balance is not compromised.
2. Timely Payment: Ensure timely payment of worker's wages & compensation.
F. Living wages: Payment of wages as per living wages, considering basic needs & provide economic security
Harassment free: Creation of workplace free of harassment & violence.

14 SDG & ESG Assuranc
Principle 4 - Respect for stakeholders' interests & responsiveness
Concept of interested party or stakeholders to a business has been a point of discussion in all regulatory
& voluntary systems that relate to mgt system of any organisation, be it related to quality, environment
or occupational health & safety of workers.
Core elements:
PDE
a. Disclose Impact: Be transparent & communicate with stakeholders about impacts of operations &
fealy decisions on people & nature. Policies, decisions & impact of operations have to be disclosed
cial
 transparently with no ambiguity on extent of issues.
SH
b. Determine Context: Determine context of operation & identify interested parties.
C. Share Benefits: Fairly share benefits to stakeholders or give opportunity to them to benefit from
noperations in equitable manner.
Aggressive: Abusive
Principle 5 - Respect & promote human rights
Covers a variety of violence & belligerent abusive issues faced by people. Refers to human rights issues
that happen directly or indirectly due to operation of business.
Core elements: UMR
Understanding: Have clear understanding of human rights & ways by which human rights can be violated
from perspective of Constitution of India, national laws & International Bill of Human Rights.
Mgt Systems: When developing mgt systems, integrate human rights element into policies, procedures
& practices.
Respect all: Recognize & respect human rights of all stakeholders & groups, including communities,
consumers & vulnerable & marginalized groups.
Principle 6 - Protection & restoration of Environment
Looks at environmental responsibility as a basic requirement for economic prosperity & sustainability.
Core elements:
olicies: Have policies, procedures & practices to assess & rectify impacts to environment. This should
over whole life cycle of product.
timum use: Make use of natural & manmade resources in optimum manner to ensure sustainability.
easure performance: Measure their performance relating to prevention of pollution, destruction of
rests, waste generation, energy use, land use, etc.
mate change initiatives: Contribute towards climate change resilience supporting India's commitment
international mechanisms like Paris Agreement & National Action Plans.
Et practices: Explore comparison of its activities with industry best practices to reduce, reuse &
ycle/ recover materials, resources.

Principle 5 - Respect & promote human rights
Covers a variety of violence & belligerent abusive issues faced by people. Refers to human rights issues
that happen directly or indirectly due to operation of business.
Core elements: UMR
a. Understanding: Havé clear understanding of human rights & ways by which human rights can be violated

b. Mgt Systems: When developing mgt systems, integrate human rights element into policies, procedures

c. Respect all: Recognize & respect human rights of all stakeholders & groups, including communities,

Principle 6 - Protection & restoration of Environment
Looks at environmental responsibility as a basic requirement for economic prosperity & sustainability.
Core elements:
a. Policies: Have policies, procedures & practices to assess & rectify impacts to environment. This should
cover whole life cycle of product.
b. Optimum use: Make use of natural & manmade resources in optimum manner to ensure sustainability.
c. Measure performance: Measure their performance relating to prevention of pollution, destruction of
forests, waste generation, energy use, land use, etc.
d. Climate change initiatives: Contribute towards climate change resilience supporting India's commitment
to international mechanisms like Paris Agreement & National Action Plans.
e. Best practices: Explore comparison of its activities with industry best practices to reduce, reuse &
recycle/ recover materials, resources.
14.4
14 SDG & ESG Assurance
Lear
With
Fun
Principle - Influence on Public & Regulatory Policy
Positively recognizes that businesses operate within the framework of statutory & legislative policies
of governing authority.
Principle further highlights that -
a.Org, should contribute to policy formulation & policy advocacy.
. Collective associations such as, trade groups & industry chambers to be utilized when moving ahead
with policy advocacy & formulation.
c. Role in policy advocacy by organisation should be in such a way that it encourages fair competition 
prevents human rights abuses.
Principle 8 - Promote Inclusive Growth & equitable development
This can work only with close participation & collaboration amongst entities, authorities, civi
associations contributing to one another for a better livelihood, & assistance to marginalized
communities.
Core elements of the eighth principle are:
a. Systems: Have systems to identify & address impacts of activities on social, cultural, & economi
aspects of people. This includes business created issues like, land acquisition, use & construction
activities for new facilities.
b. Adverse impacts: Review, measure & track adverse impacts of their activities on society & environmen
& make action plans to mitigate them adequately.
c. Creativity: Make efforts to bring up creative products, technologies & business concerns that hel
marginalized communities to have well-being & better quality of life.
d. CSR: When designing CSR activities review local & regional development priorities to help marginalize
groups & communities.
e. Displacement of Communities: Ensure that displacement or relocation of communities doesn't happen
in unavoidable cases, provide fair compensation to affected people.
Fine
Principle 9 - Provide value to consumers in responsible manner
Primary purpose of business is to create or provide useful products & services to customer fo
reasonable profits.
Core elements associated with the principle are:
Univerfal GIFT
a. Reduce Negative Impact: Put in efforts to reduce negative impacts of products & services
consumers, natural environment & society.
b. Freedom of choice: When conceptualizing, designing & marketing their products, org shouldn't preve
freedom of choice & fair competition.
c. Transparency: Transparently & accurately disclose all kinds of adverse impacts to user, planet, societ
on biodiversity from their products.
14.5
14 SDG & ESG Assurance

. Role in policy advocacy by organisation should be in such a way that it encourages fair competition &
prevents human rights abuses.
Principle 8 - Promote Inclusive Growth & equitable development
This can work only with close participation & collaboration amongst entities, authorities, civil
associations contributing to one another for a better livelihood, & assistance to marginalized
communities.
Core elements of the eighth principle are:
. Systems: Have systems to identify & address impacts of activities on social, cultural, & economic
aspects of people. This includes business created issues like, land acquisition, use & construction
activities for new facilities.
. Adverse impacts: Review, measure & track adverse impacts of their activities on society & environment
& make action plans to mitigate them adequately.
- Creativity: Make efforts to bring up creative products, technologies & business concerns that help
marginalized communities to have well-being & better quality of life.
CSR: When designing CSR activities review local & regional development priorities to help marginalized
groups & communities.
Displacement of Communities: Ensure that displacement or relocation of communities doesn't happen &
in unavoidable cases, provide fair compensation to affected people.
Fine
Principle 9 - Provide value to consumers in responsible manner
Primary purpose of business is to create or provide useful products & services to customer for
reasonable profits.
Core elements associated with the principle are:
Univerfal GIFT
Reduce Negative Impact: Put in efforts to reduce negative impacts of products & services on
consumers, natural environment & society.
Freedom of choice: When conceptualizing, designing & marketing their products, org shouldn't prevent
freedom of choice & fair competition.
Transparency: Transparently & accurately disclose all kinds of adverse impacts to user, planet, society.
on biodiversity from their products.

Assurance in BRSR
 ICAI has recently issued Standard on Sustainability Assurance Engagements (SSAE) 3000 Assurance
Engagements on Sustainability Info. Applicable to all assurance engg. on Sustainability info.
Intended users of this Standard include:
Assurance providers providing assurance on sustainability info.
Entities seeking to engage a professional auditor.
Regulators, investors, & other users of Sustainability Reporting data.
This Std. applies for assurance engg. providing reasonable or limited assurance on sustainability info.
Effective date of application of SSAE 3000:
Voluntary basis for assurance reports covering periods ending on 31.03.23.
Mandatory basis for assurance reports covering periods ending on or after 31.3.24.
ICAI has also issued SSAE 3410, Assurance Engg. on Greenhouse Gas Statements which deal with
assurance engg. on entity's sustainability info, including assurance of BRSR.
Methodology to provide assurance on BRSR
eliminary Review of ESG report, parameters
n-site Assessment / Verification of ESG Report
ubmission of findings of onsite assessment & document review
view of responses & clarifications on findings
eparation of Assessment/Verification report including results of Assessment/ Recommendation
Tuance of Assessment Report & Assessment Statement

Role of Auditor - Consideration of Climate Related Risks in Audit of FS
Role of auditor is to obtain RA whether FS are free from MM, due to fraud/error, to report whether
FS are prepared in all material respects as per AFRF.
In understanding entity, consider climate related risks & how these risks relevant to audits.

 Many investors & stakeholders are seeking info from audit reports about how climate related risks

 Audit report is communication to users about audit. In addition to opinion, it provides info about

how they were addressed.

Global Trends in Sustainable Reporting
Mandatory reporting mostly associated with public sector or govt run companies, large corporations,
MNCs, & listed cos. in stock exchange.
Most widely used framework in world is Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Sustainability Reporting
Standards having 93% of world's largest 250 corporates doing sustainability reporting through GRI. It
is used in over 100 countries. This independent international org. is based in Amsterdam, Netherlands,
& operations in Brazil, China, Colombia, India, South Africa, and US.
Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) - helps org. to report on economic, environmental, and social impacts_
General disclosures required under this std are Economic, Environment and Social. Report is addressed
to all stakeholders of entity.
2. Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP) - captures environmental performance data related to GHG emissions
water, forests, and supply chain. Major details reported are climate change, Forest, and Water
security. Addressed to all investors, buyers, and other stakeholders of entity.
. International Integrated Reporting Framework (IIRC) - Guiding principles & content elements to allow
companies to produce integrated reports. This report consists of Organisational overview, Governance
structure, Business model, risks and opportunities, strategy, performance, outlook etc.

 to be implemented to mitigate risk.
Savings in time, cost & human effort which can be utilized towards more productive tasks.

Advantages of Digital Audit
i. Enhanced Effectiveness & Efficiency: With use of tools & automation, standardized processes & routine
tasks can be automated increasing efficiency & saves time & costs.
ii. Better Analytics: Improved analytics capabilities can aid mgt & auditors in seeing trends & patterns
that may be challenging to spot manually.
iii. Improved Risk Assessment: Mgt & auditors put their testing efforts on sites with higher RoMM & make
informed decisions.
iv. Better Audit Quality: Technology can quickly & correctly evaluate massive volumes of data. This can
assist in determining areas that require more testing.
v. Lower Costs: By automating manual processes, technology can assist with lowering cost of auditing.
Consideration & Challenges of Digital Audit
Some considerations that org. should keep in mind while using digital techniques & automation:
Know what business benefits organization wants to achieve with automation
Think people first and do not underestimate change is difficult
Target the right processes - this is a key for successful automation.
Ensure process works & is standardised before automating.
Automation is not a standalone solution & should be part of a broader digitalization strategy.
Automation introduces new challenges for organization. Don't forget about governance & data security
in the risk framework.

i. Enhanced Effectiveness & Efficiency: With use of tools & automation, standardized processes & routine
tasks can be automated increasing efficiency & saves time & costs.

Improved Efficiency: What used to take weeks to learn & programme using deep experts, is now easily

vi. Better risk assessment: Focus on real challenges & assess potential risk precisely. Dashboards, visual
presentations & other tools helps in understanding where the risk lies & what areas need attention.
 
Considerations in Auditing Digitally (Using digital tools)
While all industry sectors are affected by emergence of new technologies its important to remember
that the auditor's needs are unique. There are few questions it is important to ask and answer:
Q1 What problems are you trying to solve?
Continuously evaluate emerging technologies & latest tools to see what can benefit audit.
Think about what would make audit easier/better & how you will measure your ROI.
Q2 Which tech can help you?
There are no. of tools available & many vendors & start-ups using data acquisition, manipulation &
visualization tools.
Consider how comfortably these solutions will integrate in your current processes & flag any potential
implementation issues early on.
Q3 How will you upskill your people to make best use of tech available?
Tech is only as good as people using it.
 Training & development are critical to ensure teams understand how & why they are using the tech.
however continuous training help them to get better.
iii. Increases Transparency: New ERPs & tools have audit trail feature to trace transaction end to end.
iv. Automation & Ease: Automating tasks like recording work, extracting data & sampling have improved
audit quality & reduced manual error.
V.

vi. Better risk assessment: Focus on real challenges & assess potential risk precisely. Dashboards, visual
presentations & other tools helps in understanding where the risk lies & what areas need attention.

Malware: Malware or malicious software is any program or code created with in  to harm a computer,
network or server.
Types:
1. Ransomware: Adversary encrypts victim's data & offers to provide decryption key for payment.
2. Fileless Malware
A malicious activity that uses native, legitimate tools built into a system to execute a cyber-attack,
Unlike traditional malware, fileless malware doesn't require an attacker to install any code on a target's
system, making it hard to detect.
3. Trojan
It's a malware that appears to be legitimate software disguised as native operating system programs
or harmless files like free downloads.
4. Mobile Malware
Type of malware designed to target mobile devices. Its delivered through malicious downloads,
operating system vulnerabilities, phishing, smishing, & use of unsecured Wi-Fi.
Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack is a malicious, targeted attack that floods a network with false requests
to disrupt business operations.
In a DoS attack, users are unable to perform routine & necessary tasks, such as accessing email,
websites, online a/cs or other resources that are operated by a compromised computer or network.
Ο While most DoS attacks don't result in lost data & typically resolved without paying a ransom, they cost
organization time, money & other resources to restore critical business operations.
PHISHING is a type of cyberattack that uses email, SMS, phone, social media, & social engineering
techniques to entice a victim to share sensitive info. - such as passwords or a/c nos. - or download a
malicious file that will install viruses on their computer or phone.
Types:
1. Ransomware: Adversary encrypts victim's data & offers to provide decryption key for payment.
2. Fileless Malware
A malicious activity that uses native, legitimate tools built into a system to execute a cyber-attack.
Unlike traditional malware, fileless malware doesn't require an attacker to install any code on a target's
system, making it hard to detect.
3. Trojan
It's a malware that appears to be legitimate software disguised as native operating system programs
or harmless files like free downloads.
4. Mobile Malware
 
Type of malware designed to target mobile devices. Its delivered through malicious downloads,
operating system vulnerabilities, phishing, smishing, & use of unsecured Wi-Fi.
Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack is a malicious, targeted attack that floods a network with false requests
to disrupt business operations.
In a DoS attack, users are unable to perform routine & necessary tasks, such as accessing email,
websites, online a/cs or other resources that are operated by a compromised computer or network.
While most DoS attacks don't result in lost data & typically resolved without paying a ransom, they cost
organization time, money & other resources to restore critical business operations.
PHISHING is a type of cyberattack that uses email, SMS, phone, social media, & social engineering
techniques to entice a victim to share sensitive info. - such as passwords or a/c nos. - or download a
malicious file that will install viruses on their computer or phone
Types:
1. Domain Spoofing is a form of phishing where attacker impersonates a known business or person with
fake website or email domain to fool people into trusting them. Typically, domain appears to be
legitimate at 1st glance, but closer look reveal subtle differences.
2. Email Spoofing is a type of cyberattack that targets businesses by using emails with forged sender
addresses. Because recipient trusts alleged sender, they are more likely to open the email and interact
with its contents, such as a malicious link or attachment.
Identity-Based Attacks: When a valid user's credentials been compromised & adversary pretend to be
that user. For eg, people use same user ID & password across multiple accounts. Therefore, possessing
credentials for one account may be able to grant access to other, unrelated account.
Insider Threats: When current or former employees that pose danger to an org. because they have
direct access to Co. network, sensitive data, and intellectual property (IP), as well as knowledge of
business processes, company policies or other info, that would help carry out such an attack.
DNS Tunneling is a type of cyberattack that leverages domain name system (DNS) queries & responses
to bypass traditional security measures & transmit data & code within network.
This tunnel gives hacker a route to unleash malware & extract data, IP or other sensitive info. by
encoding it bit by bit in a series of DNS responses.
IoT-Based Attack is any cyberattack that targets an Internet of Things (IoT) device or network. Once
compromised, hacker can take control of device, steal data, or join a group of infected devices
raming mo vare or other harmful software on the a

☐ Q. CA Y is planning to use CAATs extensively in audit of a Co. Be it for compliance tests or substantive
tests. Can you list out eg. Of few situations of tests performed by him using CAATS?
■ Identify exceptions: Identify exceptional transactions based on set criteria. For eg, cash transactions
above 10,000.
Identify errors: Identify data, which is inconsistent or erroneous. For e.g.: $a/c$ number which is not
numeric.
Verify calculations: Re-perform various computations in audit software to confirm results from
application software confirm with audit software. For e.g.: TDS rate applied as per criteria.
Data completeness: Identify whether all fields have valid data. For example: null values in any key field
such as date, invoice number or value or name.
Data consistency: Identify data, which are not consistent with the regular format. For eg: invoices
which are not in the required sequence.

location. Creating a new path for incoming payments that may rely on new service provider.
Auditors need to consider volume of those transactions, processes & controls related to it.
Control Testing
Shift to connected devices & systems may result in auditors unable to rely only on manual controls.
Instead, auditors may need to scope new systems into their audit.
Need to train & upskill auditors to evaluate design & operating effectiveness of automated controls.
Common risks
Device hijacking, data siphoning, denial of service attacks, data breaches and device theft.
2. Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Ο
 
Refers to system or machine that can think and learn.
AI systems utilize data analysis & algorithms to make decisions based on predictive methods.
Complex algos are developed to propose decisions based on a pattern or behaviour learned over time.
Examples:
✓ Self-driving cars, manufacturing robots, smart assistants, virtual travel booking agent etc.
✓ AI predicting when to book lowest prices for flights, hotels, car & vacation home rentals.
Using historical flight and hotel data, AI will also recommend to user whether booking has reached its
lowest price point or if user should hold out a bit longer for price to drop.
Auditor Implications
Focus: Given invisible nature of algos, audits must focus on logical flow of processes.
Ο
 
Eg. payment processing tools allowing users to pay via credit card through mobile device at retail
location. Creating a new path for incoming payments that may rely on new service provider.
Auditors need to consider volume of those transactions, processes & controls related to it.
Control Testing
Shift to connected devices & systems may result in auditors unable to rely only on manual controls.
 Instead, auditors may need to scope new systems into their audit.
Need to train & upskill auditors to evaluate design & operating effectiveness of automated controls.
Common risks
Device hijacking, data siphoning, denial of service attacks, data breaches and device theft.
2. Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Ο
Refers to system or machine that can think and learn.
AI systems utilize data analysis & algorithms to make decisions based on predictive methods.
 Complex algos are developed to propose decisions based on a pattern or behaviour learned over time.
Examples:
✓ Self-driving cars, manufacturing robots, smart assistants, virtual travel booking agent etc.
✓ AI predicting when to book lowest prices for flights, hotels, car & vacation home rentals.
Using historical flight and hotel data, AI will also recommend to user whether booking has reached its
lowest price point or if user should hold out a bit longer for price to drop.
3. Blockchain
Ο
Based on a decentralized & distributed ledger secured through encryption.
Each transaction is validated by blockchain participants, creating a block of info, that's replicated &
distributed to all participants.
All blocks are sequenced so that any modification or deletion of block disqualifies the info.
Example
Bitcoin, cryptocurrency transfer application Blockchain in money transfer, blockchain smart
contracts.
Audit Implications
Governance & Security: Consider appropriate governance & security measures around transactions.
✓ Blockchain's core security premise rests on cryptography, there are risks associated with it.
✓ As blockchain interacts with legacy systems & business partners, concerns related to insecure
application programming interfaces (APIs), data confidentiality & privacy can't be ignored.
Weak blockchain application development protocols are something auditors cannot overlook.
Data Privacy Laws: Data privacy L/R may be area of concern as data communicated across geographic
boundaries. Determine whether data put on blockchain will expose entity to liability for NOCLAR.
Common risks for blockchain technology (Strengths of blockchain can also be its weaknesses)
✓ Inability to reverse transactions & access data without required keys make system secure
✓ But also mean org. need specific protocols & processes to ensure they aren't locked out & have
contingency plans.
•
3. Blockchain
 
Based on a decentralized & distributed ledger secured through encryption.
Each transaction is validated by blockchain participants, creating a block of info. that's replicated &
distributed to all participants.
All blocks are sequenced so that any modification or deletion of block disqualifies the info.
Example
Bitcoin, cryptocurrency transfer application Blockchain in money transfer, blockchain smart
-
contracts.
Audit Implications
Governance & Security: Consider appropriate governance & security measures around transactions.
✓ Blockchain's core security premise rests on cryptography, there are risks associated with it.
✓ As blockchain interacts with legacy systems & business partners, concerns related to insecure
application programming interfaces (APIs), data confidentiality & privacy can't be ignored.
Weak blockchain application development protocols are something auditors cannot overlook.
Data Privacy Laws: Data privacy L/R may be area of concern as data communicated across geographic
boundaries. Determine whether data put on blockchain will expose entity to liability for NOCLAR.
Common risks for blockchain technology (Strengths of blockchain can also be its weaknesses)
✓ Inability to reverse transactions & access data without required keys make system secure
✓ But also mean org. need specific protocols & processes to ensure they aren't locked out & have
contingency plans.
✓ Operating through network nodes also expose org. to cyber-attacks and data hacks, so security issues
are important.
 
Exchange - NFT exchanges take place with cryptos such as Bitcoin on specialist sites.
Challenges of NFT:
Challenges like ownership & copyright concerns, security risks, market isn't that wide, online frauds
etc.
NFT audit considerations includes comprehensive code review for verifying safety of token, valid
contract, data privacy and potential cyber threat.
5. Robotic Process Automation
Ο
RPA is automation of repetitive processes performed by users.
Its a software tech that emulate humans' actions interacting with digital systems & software.
Process efficiency, customer experience & control effectiveness contributed to RPA.
RPA software bots can interact with any application or system same way people do, except that RPA
bots can operate around the clock, nonstop, faster & with 100% reliability & precision.
Audit Implications
✓ It is of utmost importance to understand RPA processes, which include data extraction, aggregation,
sanitization & cleansing. Unless auditors understand, they will not be in a position to initiate audit.
✓ A comprehensive assurance process might demand review of the source code.
✓ To perform substantive testing, auditors must have an understanding of tools used to develop &
maintain RPA.
✓ This will be helpful when auditors review logs, configuration controls, privileged access controls & the
like.
Common Risks of RPA
Operational and execution risks:
 Exchange - NFT exchanges take place with cryptos such as Bitcoin on specialist sites.
 
Challenges of NFT:
Challenges like ownership & copyright concerns, security risks, market isn't that wide, online frauds
etc.
NFT audit considerations includes comprehensive code review for verifying safety of token, valid
contract, data privacy and potential cyber threat.

As they address challenge of assessing technology risk, auditors can & should focus on following control
considerations:
1. Auditors should gain holistic understanding of changes in industry & IT environment to effectively
evaluate mgt's process for initiating, processing, & recording transactions & then design appropriate
auditing procedures.
2. Auditors should consider risks resulting from implementation of new technologies & how those risks
may differ from those that arise from more traditional, legacy systems.
3. Consider whether digital upskilling or specialists are necessary to determine impact of new technologies
& assist in risk assessment & understanding of design, implementation, and operating effectiveness of
controls. Eg, cybersecurity control experts, IT specialists in team etc.
Examples of technology risks where auditors should test
appropriate controls for relying on digital systems
Reliance on systems/programs that inaccurately process data, process inaccurate data, or both
Unauthorized access to data that might result in destruction or improper changes to data
Unauthorized or erroneous changes to data in master files
Unauthorized changes to systems or programs
Potential loss of data or inability to access data as required
Inappropriate manual intervention
Key Steps for Auditors in a Changing Technology Environment
As auditors obtain understanding of impact of technology on a Co's business, its systems of internal
control, and financial reporting, some important reminders include the following:
Maintain sufficient professional skepticism when reviewing mgt's risk assessment for new systems.

Career site pulls information of candidate.
An algorithm scans applicants for desired and suitable roles.
Selected candidates may be asked to play online games to assess their skills.
Certain percentage of applicants called for a video interview using interview software.
Automated hiring process will reduce full time effort involvement, provide with a wider assessment
range, reduce impact of recruiter biases, increase the efficiency of mapping of interested candidates,
reduce recruiting costs, increase hire yield, reduce time to hire, increase diversity.
From → To
Sampling → Full Population analysis
Multiple Datasets  One dataset

Disconnected tools → Integrated ecosystem services
Manual risk assessment  Dynamic, data driven risk assessment
Separated communication → Embedded communication
Repetitive tasks → High value work & capacity for growth

automation
Ad hoc insights → Insights from broader audit

examine count of large quantities of fixed assets & inventory.

technology to combine & extend reach and use of Cryptos, AI, AR and VR.
Some considerations for future
ר
Beyond cryptocurrencies, coins, & exchanges, players in Metaverse will have to consider how to build
digital monetary systems & apply economic principles to digital land.
Governance models will become ever more difficult to balance openness & user contribution with
strategic direction & innovation.
Identity in digital world has historically been different based on the platform utilized. Practical
challenge of identity also to be considered in Metaverse (e.g., KYC)
Synchronicity is ability for aspects of Metaverse to be multiplayer, simultaneous, & real-time. This
includes transactions & actions happening in Metaverse & are dependent on infrastructure of digital
economies, networking & computing power required to operate a digital world.
Case scenarios to illustrate potential application of metaverse in financial domain
Virtual Banking and Transactions: Forward-thinking financial institution, establishes a presence in
metaverse to offer virtual banking services. Users can create virtual bank a/c, access personalized
financial dashboards & perform transactions using virtual currencies.
Digital Asset mgt: A digital asset mgt Co, launch a virtual asset trading platform within metaverse, to
buy, sell, & trade NFTs and other digital assets.
Virtual Financial Education and Training: Financial Learning Academy (FLA) aims to enhance financial
literacy using metaverse. Create virtual classroom environment.
Virtual Meetings and Conferences: For a leading industry event, organisation hosts virtual conference
within metaverse. Participants around the world can access conference through their virtual avatars.
Data Visualization and Analytics: Co. utilizes metaverse to offer advanced data visualization and
analytics tools to financial professionals.
•
ernet technology to
allow people to have lifelike personal & business experiences online. It represents convergence of digital
technology to combine & extend reach and use of Cryptos, AI, AR and VR.
